title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.1 Introduction
When faced with multiple competing hypotheses or models, scientists need a principled way to compare them in light of observed data. The Bayesian framework provides an elegant and automatic way to do this: the posterior odds ratio, built from the Bayes factor and the prior odds. Unlike frequentist hypothesis tests that focus on rejecting a single null hypothesis, Bayesian model comparison directly assigns probabilities to models and penalizes unjustified complexity.
The key insight—Occam’s razor—emerges automatically from the mathematics. Complex models that require fine-tuning of their parameters to fit data are penalized, while simple models that make robust predictions across a wide range of parameter values are favored. This chapter develops the theory and practice of Bayesian model selection, from the basic posterior odds formula to sophisticated applications in scientific inference.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
section: 5.2 Posterior odds and the Bayes factor
text:
5.2 Posterior odds and the Bayes factor
Suppose we have two mutually exclusive and exhaustive hypotheses \(H_0\) and \(H_1\), and we observe data \(D\). Bayes’ theorem tells us:
\[P(H_i | D) = \frac{P(D | H_i) P(H_i)}{P(D)}\]
where \(P(D) = P(D | H_0) P(H_0) + P(D | H_1) P(H_1)\) is the total probability of the data, obtained by marginalizing over both hypotheses.

The posterior odds ratio is the ratio of the posterior probabilities:
\[R = \frac{P(H_1 | D)}{P(H_0 | D)}\]
This directly quantifies which hypothesis is more likely given the data. Values \(R > 1\) favor \(H_1\), while \(R < 1\) favor \(H_0\).

Now divide the posterior for \(H_1\) by the posterior for \(H_0\):
\[R = \frac{P(H_1 | D)}{P(H_0 | D)} = \frac{P(D | H_1)}{P(D | H_0)} \cdot \frac{P(H_1)}{P(H_0)}\]
The first ratio is the Bayes factor; the second is the prior odds.

The Bayes factor is:
\[\text{BF}_{10} = \frac{P(D | H_1)}{P(D | H_0)}\]
It expresses how much the data shifts the odds between the two models, independent of prior beliefs.

Thus we have the fundamental equation of Bayesian model comparison:
\[R = \text{BF}_{10} \times \frac{P(H_1)}{P(H_0)}\]

Under uniform priors—when \(P(H_1) = P(H_0) = 1/2\)—the posterior odds equal the Bayes factor. This is why some practitioners use \(\text{BF} > 1\) as a criterion for favoring \(H_1\) “objectively,” removing apparent subjectivity of the prior. However, this merely replaces one prior assumption (about models) with another (equal prior probability).
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.3 Interpreting the Bayes factor
Jeffreys (1961) proposed a scale for interpreting the magnitude of a Bayes factor:



\(\text{BF}_{10}\)
Interpretation




\(1\) to \(3\)
Weak evidence for \(H_1\)


\(3\) to \(10\)
Moderate evidence for \(H_1\)


\(10\) to \(30\)
Strong evidence for \(H_1\)


\(30\) to \(100\)
Very strong evidence for \(H_1\)


\(> 100\)
Decisive evidence for \(H_1\)



(Reverse the fractions for evidence against \(H_1\).)

This scale is somewhat arbitrary and context-dependent. In practice, consider not just the magnitude but also the uncertainty in the Bayes factor and the scientific implications of being wrong.

The posterior probabilities of the hypotheses are recovered from the odds:
\[P(H_0 | D) = \frac{1}{1 + R}, \quad P(H_1 | D) = \frac{R}{1 + R}\]
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
section: 5.4 The marginal likelihood and Occam’s razor
text:
5.4 The marginal likelihood and Occam’s razor
The Bayes factor depends on the marginal likelihood (also called the model evidence), the probability of the data averaged over all possible parameter values under that model:
\[P(D | H_i) = \int P(D | \theta, H_i) \pi(\theta | H_i) \, d\theta\]
Here \(\pi(\theta | H_i)\) is the prior distribution over parameters under hypothesis \(H_i\), and \(P(D | \theta, H_i)\) is the likelihood.

Marginal likelihoods automatically encode a penalty for model complexity. A complex model with many parameters typically spreads its prior mass over a large region of parameter space. Only a small region of this parameter space will fit the data well. When we integrate over all parameters, the contribution from the region that fits data is small relative to the total prior volume, yielding a low marginal likelihood—even if the model achieves a higher best-fit likelihood than a simpler competitor.

To see this concretely, consider two models: - \(H_0\): a simple model with a fixed parameter value \(\theta_0\). - \(H_1\): a complex model with parameter \(\theta\) uniformly distributed over the range \([a, b]\).
If both models fit the data equally well at their best-fit parameter values, then \(P(D | \theta_0, H_0) \approx P(D | \hat{\theta}, H_1)\) where \(\hat{\theta}\) is the MLE under \(H_1\).
But: \[P(D | H_0) = P(D | \theta_0, H_0)\] \[P(D | H_1) \approx \frac{1}{b-a} \int_a^b P(D | \theta, H_1) \, d\theta \approx P(D | \hat{\theta}, H_1) \cdot \frac{\Delta\theta_{\text{eff}}}{b-a}\]
where \(\Delta\theta_{\text{eff}}\) is the effective width of the likelihood peak. If \(\Delta\theta_{\text{eff}} \ll b - a\), then \(P(D | H_1) \ll P(D | H_0)\) despite \(H_1\) fitting the data equally well locally. The Bayes factor penalizes \(H_1\) for having a large prior parameter space most of which is inconsistent with the data.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
section: 5.5 Laplace approximation and the Occam factor
text:
5.5 Laplace approximation and the Occam factor
For models with continuous parameters, we can approximate the marginal likelihood using Laplace’s approximation. Assume the likelihood \(P(D | \theta, H)\) is sharply peaked around its maximum \(\hat{\theta}\), and the prior is smooth on the scale of the likelihood width.
Then: \[P(D | H) \approx P(D | \hat{\theta}, H) \cdot \pi(\hat{\theta}) \cdot \Delta\theta_{\text{eff}}\]

The Occam factor is:
\[\text{Occam} = \pi(\hat{\theta}) \cdot \Delta\theta_{\text{eff}}\]
It represents the prior probability of the region of parameter space that actually fits the data. For a model with \(d\) parameters and roughly uniform prior over range \(L\), this is approximately \((L_1 \cdots L_d)^{-1}\) if the likelihood width is much smaller than the prior width in each dimension.

The Bayes factor becomes: \[\text{BF}_{10} = \frac{P(D | \hat{\theta}_1, H_1) \cdot \text{Occam}_1}{P(D | \hat{\theta}_0, H_0) \cdot \text{Occam}_0}\]
The first term (ratio of best-fit likelihoods) favors the more flexible model. The second term (ratio of Occam factors) penalizes it for complexity. The balance determines the winner—a principle arising purely from integration, not from any ad-hoc penalty function.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.6 Example: Testing coin fairness
This classic example from Sivia (2006) illustrates the full machinery. Suppose we flip a coin \(n = 250\) times and observe \(h = 140\) heads. We compare:

\(H_0\): The coin is fair (\(\theta = 0.5\)).
\(H_1\): The coin has unknown bias \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(1,1)\) (uniform on \([0,1]\)).

The likelihood for \(h\) heads in \(n\) flips is: \[P(D | \theta) = \binom{n}{h} \theta^h (1-\theta)^{n-h}\]
Under \(H_0\): \[P(D | H_0) = \binom{n}{h} (0.5)^n\]
Under \(H_1\), with the Beta(1,1) prior: \[P(D | H_1) = \int_0^1 \binom{n}{h} \theta^h (1-\theta)^{n-h} \, d\theta = \binom{n}{h} \cdot \frac{h! (n-h)!}{(n+1)!}\]
Therefore: \[\text{BF}_{10} = \frac{P(D | H_1)}{P(D | H_0)} = \frac{h! (n-h)!}{(n+1)!} \cdot 2^n\]
With \(n = 250\) and \(h = 140\): \[\text{BF}_{10} = \frac{140! \cdot 110!}{251!} \cdot 2^{250} \approx 0.044\]
This strong evidence against the coin being fair makes intuitive sense: observing 140 heads in 250 flips is notably different from 50-50 randomness.

Now suppose we use a wider prior for \(H_1\): \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \alpha)\) for some \(\alpha > 1\). This concentrates the prior more around \(\theta = 0.5\). The Occam factor shrinks, raising the Bayes factor. Conversely, using \(\alpha < 1\) (a U-shaped prior favoring extreme values) lowers it.
This illustrates a key insight: the Bayes factor is not prior-independent. However, it responds sensibly: wider priors (more agnostic) increase the Occam penalty, favoring simpler models; narrower priors (more peaked) decrease the penalty. A practitioner should explore this sensitivity and report it alongside point estimates.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.7 Lindley’s paradox
A famous puzzle arises when comparing a point null hypothesis (e.g., \(\theta = 0\)) to a broad alternative. As sample size increases, frequentist p-values eventually fall below any threshold (e.g., 0.05), calling for rejection of the null. Yet the Bayes factor may simultaneously favor the null, if the alternative prior is very diffuse.

Suppose we test \(H_0: \mu = 0\) versus \(H_1: \mu \sim N(0, \sigma_\mu^2)\) on data \(x_1, \ldots, x_n \sim N(\mu, 1)\). With a very large sample and \(\bar{x}\) only slightly away from zero, a frequentist test will reject \(H_0\) (small p-value), but if \(\sigma_\mu\) is very large (vague prior on \(\mu\)), the Occam penalty under \(H_1\) keeps the Bayes factor favoring \(H_0\).
The resolution is that the “paradox” reflects a real difference in how the two frameworks treat vague alternatives. Frequentist tests focus on the likelihood under \(H_0\). Bayesian tests also account for where the alternative prior was placed. This is not an error but a feature: it discourages chasing phantom effects in vague parameter spaces.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.8 Sensitivity analysis and recommendations
Because the Bayes factor depends on prior specification under each model, responsible practice demands a sensitivity analysis:

Vary the prior width under the complex model within a reasonable range.
Plot or tabulate the Bayes factor as a function of prior width.
Report the range of Bayes factors across plausible priors.
Use multiple Bayes factor values (e.g., the median and bounds) rather than a single point estimate.

If the Bayes factor remains in the same evidential category across a wide range of prior specifications, the conclusion is robust. If it changes materially, the inference is prior-sensitive and deserves careful discussion.

Some software implementations use automatic prior calibration rules (e.g., based on effect size or information content in the data) to reduce arbitrariness. While convenient, these methods still embed assumptions. Transparency about prior choice is always preferable to the illusion of objectivity.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
section: 5.9 Brief comparison with frequentist hypothesis testing
text:
5.9 Brief comparison with frequentist hypothesis testing
The frequentist approach to hypothesis testing is conceptually different. It treats parameters as fixed but unknown and focuses on controlling error rates across repeated hypothetical samples:

A test statistic \(T(D)\) is compared to a threshold derived from the null distribution \(P(T | H_0)\). The p-value is \(P(T(D) \geq t_{\text{obs}} | H_0)\), the probability of observing data as or more extreme than observed, if \(H_0\) were true. A p-value below \(\alpha\) (e.g., 0.05) triggers rejection of \(H_0\).

Key differences:








Feature
Bayesian
Frequentist




Assigns probability to hypotheses
Yes
No; tests hypothesis rejection rates


Uses prior information
Yes; explicitly
No; aims for objectivity through error control


Penalizes complexity
Automatically via Occam factor
Only through significance level (weak link)


Interpretation of p-value
N/A
Probability of data under \(H_0\), not probability \(H_0\) is true


Sample size effects
Accounted for naturally
Strong: small effects are rejected in large samples, regardless of effect size


Binary decision
Unnecessary; provides probabilities
Often yes/no; less flexible



Both frameworks are useful and address different questions. Frequentist tests excel at controlling false-positive rates in exploratory settings. Bayesian methods excel when you want to rank competing theories and make decisions balancing evidence and consequences. Modern practice often combines both: use frequentist tests for initial screening, then Bayesian analysis for focused inference.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.10 Computational aspects
Computing the marginal likelihood \(P(D | H)\) is often the bottleneck in Bayesian model comparison. For simple models (coin flips, Gaussian data), closed-form solutions exist. For complex likelihoods and high-dimensional parameter spaces, numerical methods are essential:

Laplace approximation: Fast, requires only the posterior mode and Hessian matrix.
Markov Chain Monte Carlo: Generates posterior samples; marginal likelihood can be estimated using harmonic mean or bridge sampling.
Nested sampling: Efficient algorithm specifically designed for evidence computation.
Variational inference: Approximate posterior, often with tractable marginal likelihood.

The choice depends on model complexity and available computational resources. For pedagogical purposes, we focus on settings where analytical solutions or Laplace approximation suffice.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.11 Examples and visualization


Show code
# Coin fairness example: n = 250 flips, h = 140 heads
# H0: theta = 0.5 (fair coin)
# H1: theta ~ Beta(alpha, alpha) with varying alpha

n <- 250
h <- 140

# Likelihood under H0
log_likelihood_H0 <- h * log(0.5) + (n - h) * log(0.5) + lchoose(n, h)

# Marginal likelihood under H1 with Beta(alpha, alpha) prior
# For Beta-Binomial model: P(D|H1) = C(n,h) * Beta(h+alpha, n-h+alpha) / Beta(alpha, alpha)
bayes_factor_beta_binomial <- function(n, h, alpha) {
  log_ll_H1 <- lchoose(n, h) +
    lbeta(h + alpha, n - h + alpha) -
    lbeta(alpha, alpha)
  exp(log_ll_H1 - log_likelihood_H0)
}

# Grid of prior concentrations
alpha_values <- seq(0.5, 50, by = 0.5)
bf_values <- sapply(alpha_values, function(a) bayes_factor_beta_binomial(n, h, a))

# Three-tone palette
col_bf <- "#1f77b4"      # Blue for BF curve
col_guide <- "#ff7f0e"   # Orange for prior-width guide
col_accent <- "#2ca02c"  # Green for annotations

par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
plot(alpha_values, bf_values, type = "l", lwd = 1,
     xlab = "Prior concentration (alpha)",
     ylab = "Bayes Factor (BF_10)",
     col = col_bf, ylim = c(0, max(bf_values) * 1.1))
abline(h = 1, lty = 2, col = col_guide, lwd = 0.8)
text(30, 1.05, "BF = 1 (indifference)", col = col_guide, cex = 0.85, adj = 0)

# Highlight a specific prior width
alpha_focus <- 5
bf_focus <- bayes_factor_beta_binomial(n, h, alpha_focus)
points(alpha_focus, bf_focus, pch = 16, col = col_accent, cex = 1.2)
segments(alpha_focus, 0, alpha_focus, bf_focus, lty = 3, col = col_accent, lwd = 0.8)
text(alpha_focus - 8, bf_focus + 0.15, sprintf("alpha=%.1f\nBF=%.3f", alpha_focus, bf_focus),
     col = col_accent, cex = 0.8, adj = 1)





The Bayes factor changes with the width of the prior under H1. A narrower prior (concentrated near 0.5) increases the Occam penalty, lowering the BF. A very wide prior spreads mass over the entire [0,1] interval, also increasing the penalty. An intermediate width balances fit and complexity.






Show code
# Sequential analysis: build up data gradually
n_max <- 250
h_max <- 140

# Track data proportionally
flips_seq <- seq(10, n_max, by = 10)
heads_seq <- round(flips_seq * (h_max / n_max))

# Compute Bayes factor at each stage
bf_seq <- mapply(function(n_i, h_i) {
  bayes_factor_beta_binomial(n_i, h_i, alpha = 1)
}, flips_seq, heads_seq)

# Posterior odds with uniform prior on H0 vs H1
posterior_odds <- bf_seq

par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
plot(flips_seq, posterior_odds, type = "l", lwd = 1,
     xlab = "Number of flips",
     ylab = "Posterior odds ratio (R = BF)",
     col = col_bf, log = "y")
abline(h = 1, lty = 2, col = col_guide, lwd = 0.8)
abline(h = 10, lty = 2, col = col_guide, lwd = 0.8)
text(20, 0.8, "R = 1 (equal)", col = col_guide, cex = 0.8, adj = 0)
text(20, 12, "R = 10 (strong)", col = col_guide, cex = 0.8, adj = 0)

# Mark final observation
points(n_max, posterior_odds[length(posterior_odds)], pch = 16, col = col_accent, cex = 1.2)
text(n_max - 30, posterior_odds[length(posterior_odds)] * 0.7,
     sprintf("n=250\nR=%.2e", posterior_odds[length(posterior_odds)]),
     col = col_accent, cex = 0.8, adj = 1)





Sequential Bayes factor as we collect more coin flips. With 140 heads per 250 flips, the evidence against fairness accumulates rapidly. After ~80 flips, the posterior odds strongly favor the biased model.






Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))

# Smooth likelihood function centered at theta_hat = 0.56
theta <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)
theta_hat <- 0.56
delta_theta_eff <- 0.08

# Likelihood (normalized for display)
likelihood <- exp(-((theta - theta_hat)^2) / (2 * delta_theta_eff^2))
likelihood <- likelihood / max(likelihood)

# Prior widths
prior_width_simple <- 0.1   # H0 narrow
prior_width_complex <- 0.8  # H1 wide

# Priors
prior_simple <- ifelse(theta >= 0.45 & theta <= 0.55, 1 / prior_width_simple, 0)
prior_complex <- rep(1 / 1, length(theta))  # Uniform over [0,1]

plot(theta, likelihood, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = col_bf,
     xlab = "Parameter theta",
     ylab = "Density (normalized)",
     ylim = c(0, 1.1),
     main = "")

# Draw prior widths
lines(c(0.45, 0.55), c(1.0, 1.0), col = col_accent, lwd = 3)
points(c(0.45, 0.55), c(1.0, 1.0), pch = "|", col = col_accent, cex = 2)
text(0.5, 1.05, "H0 prior width", col = col_accent, cex = 0.8, adj = 0.5)

lines(c(0, 1), c(0.85, 0.85), col = col_guide, lwd = 2)
points(c(0, 1), c(0.85, 0.85), pch = "|", col = col_guide, cex = 2)
text(0.5, 0.90, "H1 prior width", col = col_guide, cex = 0.8, adj = 0.5)

# Shade the effective width
polygon(c(theta_hat - 2*delta_theta_eff, theta_hat + 2*delta_theta_eff,
          theta_hat + 2*delta_theta_eff, theta_hat - 2*delta_theta_eff),
        c(-0.02, -0.02, 0.2, 0.2), col = rgb(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.2), border = NA)
text(theta_hat, 0.12, "Effective\nlikelihood\nwidth", cex = 0.7, adj = 0.5)

legend("topright", c("Likelihood", "H0 prior", "H1 prior"),
       col = c(col_bf, col_accent, col_guide), lwd = c(2, 3, 2),
       cex = 0.8, bg = "white")





Two models fit data equally well at their best-fit values, but the complex model’s prior is spread over a wider parameter space. The effective width of the likelihood peak (Delta_theta_eff) is the same for both, but the prior width differs. The Occam factor penalizes the complex model by the ratio of prior widths.
crumbs: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors

title: 5  Model comparison and Bayes factors
text:
5.12 Computing the marginal likelihood
Computing the marginal likelihood \(p(D \mid M) = \int p(D \mid \theta, M)\, p(\theta \mid M)\, d\theta\) is often the primary computational bottleneck in Bayesian model comparison. For simple conjugate models—where the prior and likelihood are conjugate families—analytical solutions exist. In all other settings, we must resort to numerical approximation. This section surveys the four most practical methods, comparing their assumptions, computational costs, reliability, and pitfalls.

5.12.1 Laplace approximation
The Laplace approximation provides a fast, analytic approximation to the marginal likelihood when the posterior is sharply peaked around its mode. Expanding the log-posterior around the maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate \(\hat{\theta}\) to second order and assuming the prior is smooth at the peak:

\[p(D \mid M) \approx p(D \mid \hat{\theta}, M)\, p(\hat{\theta} \mid M)\, (2\pi)^{d/2}\, |\mathcal{H}|^{-1/2}\]
where \(d\) is the dimension of the parameter space and \(\mathcal{H}\) is the Hessian of the negative log-posterior evaluated at \(\hat{\theta}\).

Strengths: Requires only the MAP and Hessian, making it computationally cheap; often accurate when the posterior is well-approximated by a high-dimensional Gaussian.
Weaknesses: Poor accuracy for multimodal posteriors, heavy-tailed posteriors, or when the likelihood is only weakly peaked. The approximation’s quality degrades in high dimensions.

For many applied problems, the Laplace approximation is a good starting point. Use it as a baseline and compare with more robust methods if possible.



5.12.2 Harmonic mean estimator
If posterior samples \(\theta^{(s)}, \, s = 1, \ldots, S\) are available from MCMC, the harmonic mean estimator offers a simple plug-in approximation:
\[\hat{Z}_{\mathrm{HM}} = \left[\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S \frac{1}{p(D \mid \theta^{(s)}, M)}\right]^{-1}\]
This estimator arises naturally as an importance-sampling approximation with the prior \(p(\theta \mid M)\) as the proposal distribution. The key idea is that the posterior samples are reweighted by the reciprocal of the likelihood, inverting the usual likelihood weighting.

Remark. Pitfall: catastrophic instability. The harmonic mean estimator has infinite variance whenever the prior has lighter tails than the posterior, which occurs in nearly every realistic setting. A single posterior sample in a region of low prior density can dominate the sum, causing enormous variance and unreliable estimates. Newton & Raftery (1994) and Wolpert & Schmidler (2012) have thoroughly documented this pathology. We strongly discourage the use of the harmonic mean estimator in production analyses. Its tempting simplicity belies a fundamental flaw.



5.12.3 Bridge sampling
Meng & Wong (1996) introduced bridge sampling, a principled method that corrects the importance-sampling bias of the harmonic mean. The method is based on the identity:
\[\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = \frac{\mathbb{E}_{q_2}[\alpha(\theta)\,q_1(\theta)]}{\mathbb{E}_{q_1}[\alpha(\theta)\,q_2(\theta)]},\]
where \(q_1\) and \(q_2\) are any two probability densities with known (or tractable) normalizing constants \(Z_1\) and \(Z_2\), and \(\alpha\) is an arbitrary “bridge” function. The optimal choice of \(\alpha\) (in the sense of minimizing asymptotic variance) is the geometric-mean form. By setting \(q_1\) to the posterior and \(q_2\) to the prior, we recover an estimate of the marginal likelihood.
Strengths: Much lower variance than the harmonic mean; scales well to moderate-to-high dimensions; widely implemented (e.g., in the R package bridgesampling of Gronau et al. 2017).
Weaknesses: Requires posterior samples and careful selection of the bridge function. Sensitivity to the proposal and the effective sample size.

Bridge sampling is the recommended default when posterior samples are already in hand (e.g., from an MCMC run) and model comparison is a secondary goal. The bridgesampling R package provides a robust, production-ready implementation.



5.12.4 Nested sampling
Skilling (2006) reformulated the marginal likelihood computation as an integration over the prior mass enclosed by likelihood contours. Define \(X(\lambda) = \int_{\{L(\theta) > \lambda\}} p(\theta \mid M)\, d\theta\) as the prior mass in regions where the likelihood exceeds \(\lambda\). Then:
\[Z = \int p(D \mid \theta, M)\, p(\theta \mid M)\, d\theta = \int_0^1 L(X)\, dX,\]
where \(L(X)\) is the likelihood as a function of enclosed prior mass \(X\).
The nested sampling algorithm maintains a set of \(K\) “live points” uniformly distributed under the prior. At each iteration, the point with the lowest likelihood is removed (added to the “dead points”), and a new point is drawn uniformly from the prior restricted to the region of higher likelihood. The marginal likelihood is accumulated as:
\[Z \approx \sum_{i} L_i \, \Delta X_i,\]
where \(L_i\) and \(\Delta X_i\) are the likelihoods and prior-mass increments of dead points.
Strengths: Handles multimodal posteriors naturally (each mode is explored independently); provides both marginal likelihood and posterior samples (via importance reweighting of dead points); increasingly popular in astrophysics and cosmology.
Weaknesses: More computationally expensive than Laplace (requires many likelihood evaluations); requires careful tuning of the number of live points and the constrained-prior sampler.

Nested sampling is implemented in MultiNest (Feroz et al.), dynesty (Speagle), and PolyChord (Handley et al.). Choose nested sampling when the posterior is complex, multimodal, or when you need both evidence and posterior samples with high confidence.



5.12.5 Thermodynamic integration and annealed importance sampling
A fourth family of methods leverages a path of intermediate distributions. Define the tempered posterior:
\[\pi_\beta(\theta) \propto p(D \mid \theta, M)^\beta\, p(\theta \mid M), \quad \beta \in [0, 1],\]
interpolating from the prior (\(\beta = 0\)) to the posterior (\(\beta = 1\)). The marginal likelihood satisfies:
\[\log Z = \int_0^1 \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\beta}\!\big[\log p(D \mid \theta, M)\big]\, d\beta.\]
This identity is the foundation of thermodynamic integration (Gelman & Meng 1998) and annealed importance sampling (Neal 2001), which numerically integrate the expected log-likelihood along the temperature path.
Strengths: Robust, applicable to high-dimensional and multimodal problems; naturally embedded in parallel-tempering MCMC schemes (Chapter 10).
Weaknesses: Requires careful choice of the temperature schedule and numerical integration scheme.


5.12.6 Comparison table










Method
When to use
Strengths
Weaknesses
Cost




Laplace
Quick baseline with smooth, unimodal posterior
Fast; only MAP and Hessian needed
Poor for multimodal or heavy-tailed posteriors
Very low


Harmonic mean
Avoid
Simplicity; uses existing MCMC samples
Catastrophic instability; infinite variance
Very low (but unreliable)


Bridge sampling
MCMC samples in hand; moderate-to-high dimensions
Low variance; theoretically principled; well-implemented
Requires tuning; sensitive to proposal
Low-to-moderate


Nested sampling
Multimodal or complex posteriors; want both \(Z\) and posterior
Handles multimodality naturally; provides full uncertainty; robust
Computationally expensive; requires tuning
Moderate-to-high


Thermodynamic integration
High-dimensional problems; parallel tempering available
Robust; scalable to high dimensions
Needs temperature schedule; numerical integration error
Moderate-to-high





5.12.7 Practical recommendations
For rapid Bayes factor assessment with a well-conditioned, unimodal posterior, compute the Laplace approximation and treat it as a baseline estimate, supplemented by uncertainty bounds. If you have posterior samples from MCMC and seek a robust point estimate, use bridge sampling—it is the practical sweet spot of accuracy, interpretability, and implementation maturity. For complex, multimodal posteriors or when maximum confidence in the evidence is essential, employ nested sampling despite its higher computational cost. Never rely on the harmonic mean estimator for production analyses.
In all cases, report not just a point estimate of the marginal likelihood but also an uncertainty band, and perform sensitivity analyses across prior specifications if feasible.

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.1 Introduction
Parameter estimation lies at the heart of Bayesian inference. Once we observe data, we want to infer the unknown parameters that generated it. Unlike frequentist approaches that treat parameters as fixed but unknown, the Bayesian framework treats parameters as random variables described by probability distributions. This chapter synthesizes three core topics: how to estimate parameters using loss functions, how to quantify uncertainty via credible intervals, and how to make predictions about future observations.
The structure follows a natural progression: we begin with the Bayesian estimation problem and loss functions, explore how to characterize posterior shapes and construct intervals, then move to prediction and compare with frequentist confidence intervals.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.2 The Bayesian estimation problem

Bayesian inference proceeds through Bayes’ theorem: \[
p(\theta \mid D, H) = \frac{p(D \mid \theta, H) \, p(\theta \mid H)}{p(D \mid H)},
\] where - \(p(D \mid \theta, H)\) is the likelihood: the probability of observing data \(D\) under parameter \(\theta\) and model \(H\), - \(p(\theta \mid H)\) is the prior: our belief about \(\theta\) before seeing data, - \(p(\theta \mid D, H)\) is the posterior: our updated belief after data, - \(p(D \mid H) = \int p(D \mid \theta, H) p(\theta \mid H) d\theta\) is the evidence (normalizing constant).

The posterior distribution contains all information about \(\theta\) given the data. Once we have it, we face a practical question: should we quote a point estimate or a full distribution?
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.3 Point estimation under loss functions
The Bayesian answer to point estimation is principled: choose an estimate by minimizing expected loss given the data. Different loss functions lead to different estimators.

Given data \(D\), the Bayesian estimator is \[
\hat{\theta}(D) = \arg\min_a E[L(a, \theta) \mid D],
\] where \(L(a, \theta)\) is a loss function penalizing how far the estimate \(a\) is from the true parameter \(\theta\).


6.3.1 Three canonical loss functions
1. Squared loss and the posterior mean
\[
L(a, \theta) = (a - \theta)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}_{\text{LMS}} = E[\theta \mid D]
\]
Minimizing squared loss yields the posterior mean (Least Mean Square estimator). Completing the square: \[
E[(a - \theta)^2 \mid D] = (a - E[\theta \mid D])^2 + \text{Var}(\theta \mid D).
\] The posterior mean uses the full posterior, is invariant under reparametrization, but can be pulled by heavy tails.
2. Absolute loss and the posterior median
\[
L(a, \theta) = |a - \theta| \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}_{\text{med}} = \text{median}(\theta \mid D)
\]
The posterior median is more robust to outliers than the mean and divides the posterior into equal probability masses.
3. 0-1 loss and the maximum a posteriori (MAP)
\[
L(a, \theta) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } a = \theta \\ 1 & \text{if } a \neq \theta \end{cases}
\quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta p(\theta \mid D)
\]
The MAP is the posterior mode — computationally convenient but uses only local information around the peak. Critically, MAP is not invariant under reparametrization: if we change variables \(\phi = g(\theta)\), the mode of \(p(\phi \mid D)\) generally does not equal \(g(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}})\).


6.3.2 Illustrating loss functions


Show code
a_values <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 500)
theta_true <- 0.7

par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1), cex.main = 0.95)

# Squared loss
L_squared <- (a_values - theta_true)^2
plot(a_values, L_squared, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#d62728",
     main = "Squared Loss", xlab = expression(a), ylab = expression(L(a, theta)))
abline(v = theta_true, lty = 2, col = "grey")
grid(col = "grey90")

# Absolute loss
L_abs <- abs(a_values - theta_true)
plot(a_values, L_abs, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#1f77b4",
     main = "Absolute Loss", xlab = expression(a), ylab = expression(L(a, theta)))
abline(v = theta_true, lty = 2, col = "grey")
grid(col = "grey90")

# 0-1 loss
L_01 <- ifelse(abs(a_values - theta_true) < 0.2, 0, 1)
plot(a_values, L_01, type = "s", lwd = 2.5, col = "#2ca02c",
     main = "0-1 Loss", xlab = expression(a), ylab = expression(L(a, theta)),
     ylim = c(-0.1, 1.2))
abline(v = theta_true, lty = 2, col = "grey")
grid(col = "grey90")





Squared loss (parabola) penalizes large errors; absolute loss (V-shape) is linear; 0-1 loss (step) treats all errors equally.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
section: 6.4 Asymmetric posteriors: MAP vs mean vs median
text:
6.4 Asymmetric posteriors: MAP vs mean vs median
When the posterior is sharply peaked and symmetric (e.g., Gaussian), the MAP, mean, and median coincide. However, realistic posteriors are often skewed or multimodal.

Bernoulli parameter estimation with small samples
Suppose we toss a coin \(n = 10\) times and observe \(k = 3\) heads. Using a uniform prior \(\text{Beta}(1, 1)\), the posterior is \(\text{Beta}(1 + 3, 1 + 7) = \text{Beta}(4, 8)\).


Show code
p <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)
posterior <- dbeta(p, shape1 = 4, shape2 = 8)

# Compute statistics
p_map <- (4 - 1) / (4 + 8 - 2)
p_mean <- 4 / (4 + 8)
p_median <- qbeta(0.5, 4, 8)

plot(p, posterior, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#d62728",
     main = "Asymmetric Beta(4,8) Posterior",
     xlab = expression(p), ylab = "Density")
abline(v = p_map, col = "#2ca02c", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
abline(v = p_median, col = "#1f77b4", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
abline(v = p_mean, col = "#9467bd", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("Posterior", "MAP", "Median", "Mean"),
       col = c("#d62728", "#2ca02c", "#1f77b4", "#9467bd"),
       lwd = c(2.5, 2, 2, 2), lty = c(1, 2, 2, 2), bty = "n")
grid(col = "grey90")





Beta(4,8) posterior (red) is skewed right. MAP (green), median (blue), and mean (purple) differ substantially.




Here, \(\hat{p}_{\text{MAP}} \approx 0.27\), \(\text{median} \approx 0.31\), and \(E[p \mid D] \approx 0.33\). The mean is pulled toward 0.5 (the neutral Beta(1,1) prior), while the MAP stays closer to the data.


6.4.1 The reparametrization invariance problem
This is a critical issue for MAP estimation. Consider the logit transformation \(\phi = \log\frac{p}{1-p}\), so \(p = \frac{e^\phi}{1 + e^\phi}\).

When we change variables, the Jacobian enters the posterior density: \[
p(\phi \mid D) = p(p \mid D) \left\vert \frac{dp}{d\phi} \right\vert = p(p \mid D) \cdot p(1-p).
\] This Jacobian modifies the exponents in the likelihood, so \(\arg\max_\phi p(\phi \mid D) \neq g(\arg\max_p p(p \mid D))\) unless the Jacobian is constant.
The posterior mean and median are invariant under reparametrization because they are defined via integration, not pointwise optimization. This makes them more principled choices.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
section: 6.5 Reliability: Gaussian approximation and Laplace’s method
text:
6.5 Reliability: Gaussian approximation and Laplace’s method
For large samples or sharply peaked posteriors, we can approximate the posterior as Gaussian using a Taylor expansion around the MAP.

Laplace’s method: If the posterior is sharply unimodal around \(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}}\), then \[
p(\theta \mid D, H) \approx N(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}}, C),
\] where the covariance matrix is \[
C = H^{-1}, \quad H_{ij} = -\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(\theta \mid D)}{\partial \theta_i \partial \theta_j}\bigg|_{\theta = \hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}}}
\] is the negative Hessian of the log-posterior.
The uncertainty in each parameter is \(\sigma_i = \sqrt{C_{ii}}\).


6.5.1 When is Laplace reliable?
The Gaussian approximation works well when: - The posterior is unimodal and symmetric. - The sample size is large (CLT effect). - Parameters stay away from natural boundaries.
It breaks down when: - Posterior is broad, skewed, or multimodal. - Data are sparse (small \(n\), far from the CLT regime). - Parameters are bounded (e.g., \(p \in [0,1]\), \(\sigma^2 > 0\)).

Bernoulli reliability
For the Bernoulli likelihood with a Beta prior, the log-posterior is \[
L(p) = (a-1) \ln p + (b-1) \ln(1-p),
\] where \(a = \alpha + k\) and \(b = \beta + n - k\).
The second derivative at the MAP is \[
L''(\hat{p}_{\text{MAP}}) = -\frac{(a+b-2)^2}{(a-1)(b-1)}.
\]
The uncertainty scales as \(\sigma \sim (n + \alpha + \beta)^{-1/2}\). For small \(n\) or extreme counts, the quadratic approximation is poor because the Beta posterior has heavy tails.



6.5.2 Gaussian approximation: good vs bad case


Show code
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# Good case: many counts
alpha_good <- 50
beta_good <- 10
lambda_grid <- seq(0, 8, length.out = 1000)
post_good <- dgamma(lambda_grid, shape = alpha_good, rate = beta_good)

map_good <- (alpha_good - 1) / beta_good
var_good <- alpha_good / (beta_good^2)
gauss_good <- dnorm(lambda_grid, mean = map_good, sd = sqrt(var_good))

plot(lambda_grid, post_good, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#d62728",
     main = "Well-peaked Gamma(50,10)", xlab = expression(lambda), ylab = "Density")
lines(lambda_grid, gauss_good, lwd = 2, col = "black", lty = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("True posterior", "Laplace approx."),
       col = c("#d62728", "black"), lwd = c(2.5, 2), lty = c(1, 2), bty = "n")
grid(col = "grey90")

# Bad case: no counts
alpha_bad <- 1
beta_bad <- 5
lambda_grid_bad <- seq(0, 2, length.out = 1000)
post_bad <- dgamma(lambda_grid_bad, shape = alpha_bad, rate = beta_bad)

map_bad <- (alpha_bad - 1) / beta_bad
var_bad <- alpha_bad / (beta_bad^2)
gauss_bad <- dnorm(lambda_grid_bad, mean = map_bad, sd = sqrt(var_bad))

plot(lambda_grid_bad, post_bad, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#d62728",
     main = "Broad Gamma(1,5) [zero counts]", xlab = expression(lambda), ylab = "Density")
lines(lambda_grid_bad, gauss_bad, lwd = 2, col = "black", lty = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("True posterior", "Laplace approx."),
       col = c("#d62728", "black"), lwd = c(2.5, 2), lty = c(1, 2), bty = "n")
grid(col = "grey90")





Left: well-peaked Gamma(50,10) posterior (n=49 counts). Right: broad Gamma(1,5) posterior (y=0 counts). Gaussian fit (black dashed) tracks poorly in the second case.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.6 Credible intervals
When the posterior is broad or asymmetric, a single point estimate with symmetric error bars is misleading. Instead, we summarize the posterior with credible intervals: regions containing a specified probability mass.

A credible interval \([\theta^-, \theta^+]\) at credibility level \(\gamma\) satisfies \[
P(\theta \in [\theta^-, \theta^+] \mid D, H) = \gamma.
\] The posterior probability that \(\theta\) lies in this interval is \(\gamma\), conditioned on the observed data.


6.6.1 Central credible intervals
The central (or equal-tail) credible interval places equal probability mass in each tail: \[
\int_{-\infty}^{\theta^-} p(\theta \mid D) d\theta = \int_{\theta^+}^{\infty} p(\theta \mid D) d\theta = \frac{1-\gamma}{2}.
\]
The endpoints are quantiles: \(\theta^\pm = F^{-1}\left(\frac{1 \pm \gamma}{2}\right)\), where \(F\) is the CDF of the posterior.
For a Gaussian posterior with mean \(\mu\) and std \(\sigma\), the 68%, 95%, and 99.7% central intervals are the familiar \(\mu \pm 1\sigma\), \(\mu \pm 1.96\sigma\), and \(\mu \pm 3\sigma\).


6.6.2 Highest Posterior Density (HPD) intervals

A Highest Posterior Density (HPD) interval at credibility level \(\gamma\) is the smallest-width region containing probability mass \(\gamma\) with the highest posterior density. Equivalently, \[
\text{HPD}_\gamma = \{\theta : p(\theta \mid D) \geq k_\gamma\},
\] where \(k_\gamma\) is the largest constant such that \(\int_{\text{HPD}_\gamma} p(\theta \mid D) d\theta = \gamma\).
Properties: - Every point inside the HPD has higher density than every point outside. - All points in the HPD have posterior density \(\geq k_\gamma\). - For symmetric distributions, HPD coincides with the central interval. - For asymmetric or multimodal posteriors, the HPD can be disconnected. - HPD is the minimum-width interval.



6.6.3 HPD construction by threshold
The practical algorithm is simple: sort posterior density values in descending order, accumulate probability mass until reaching \(\gamma\), and find the density threshold \(k_\gamma\) where this occurs. Then the HPD is the set of all \(\theta\) with \(p(\theta \mid D) \geq k_\gamma\).


Show code
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 2000)
dens <- 0.5 * dbeta(x, 2, 8) + 0.5 * dbeta(x, 8, 2)

gamma <- 0.80
dx <- x[2] - x[1]
dens_sorted <- sort(dens, decreasing = TRUE)
cum_prob <- cumsum(dens_sorted) * dx
k_gamma <- dens_sorted[which(cum_prob >= gamma)[1]]

# Central interval (for comparison)
cum_dens <- cumsum(dens) * dx
cum_dens_norm <- cum_dens / max(cum_dens)
idx_central <- which(cum_dens_norm <= (1 + gamma)/2 & cum_dens_norm >= (1 - gamma)/2)

plot(x, dens, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#1f77b4",
     main = paste0("HPD vs Central Interval (gamma = ", gamma, ")"),
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = "Posterior density")

# Shade HPD
hpd_idx <- dens >= k_gamma
polygon(c(x[hpd_idx], rev(x[hpd_idx])), c(dens[hpd_idx], rep(0, sum(hpd_idx))),
        col = rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.3), border = NA)

# Threshold line
abline(h = k_gamma, col = "red", lwd = 1.5, lty = 2)

legend("top", legend = c("Posterior density", "HPD region", expression(k[gamma])),
       col = c("#1f77b4", rgb(1,0,0,0.3), "red"),
       lwd = c(2.5, NA, 1.5), lty = c(1, NA, 2), pch = c(NA, 15, NA), bty = "n")
grid(col = "grey90")





HPD interval (red shaded) vs central credible interval (blue shaded) for a bimodal mixture of Beta distributions. HPD is disconnected; central interval includes low-density region between modes.

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
section: 6.7 Worked example: Beta posterior with equal-tail and HPD intervals
text:
6.7 Worked example: Beta posterior with equal-tail and HPD intervals
Suppose we observe \(k = 5\) heads in \(n = 20\) coin flips with a uniform prior Beta(1,1). The posterior is Beta(6, 16).


Show code
p <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)
alpha_post <- 6
beta_post <- 16
posterior <- dbeta(p, alpha_post, beta_post)

gamma <- 0.95

# Equal-tail interval
p_lower_central <- qbeta((1 - gamma) / 2, alpha_post, beta_post)
p_upper_central <- qbeta((1 + gamma) / 2, alpha_post, beta_post)

# HPD: find threshold
dens_sorted <- sort(posterior, decreasing = TRUE)
dx <- p[2] - p[1]
cum_prob <- cumsum(dens_sorted) * dx
k_gamma <- dens_sorted[which(cum_prob >= gamma)[1]]
hpd_idx <- posterior >= k_gamma

# Plot
plot(p, posterior, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#1f77b4",
     main = "Beta(6,16): Equal-tail vs HPD (95%)",
     xlab = expression(p), ylab = "Density")

# Shade equal-tail
idx_et <- (p >= p_lower_central) & (p <= p_upper_central)
polygon(c(p[idx_et], rev(p[idx_et])), c(posterior[idx_et], rep(0, sum(idx_et))),
        col = rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.2), border = NA)

# Shade HPD
polygon(c(p[hpd_idx], rev(p[hpd_idx])), c(posterior[hpd_idx], rep(0, sum(hpd_idx))),
        col = rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.25), border = NA)

# Mean and mode
p_mean <- alpha_post / (alpha_post + beta_post)
p_mode <- (alpha_post - 1) / (alpha_post + beta_post - 2)
abline(v = p_mean, col = "#9467bd", lwd = 1.5, lty = 3)
abline(v = p_mode, col = "#2ca02c", lwd = 1.5, lty = 3)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("Posterior", "Equal-tail [95%]", "HPD [95%]", "Mean", "Mode"),
       col = c("#1f77b4", rgb(0,0,1,0.2), rgb(1,0,0,0.25), "#9467bd", "#2ca02c"),
       lwd = c(2.5, NA, NA, 1.5, 1.5), lty = c(1, NA, NA, 3, 3),
       pch = c(NA, 15, 15, NA, NA), bty = "n")

cat("Equal-tail:  [", sprintf("%.4f", p_lower_central), ",", sprintf("%.4f", p_upper_central), "]\n", sep = "")


Equal-tail:  [0.1128,0.4717]


Show code
cat("HPD:         [", sprintf("%.4f", min(p[hpd_idx])), ",", sprintf("%.4f", max(p[hpd_idx])), "]\n", sep = "")


HPD:         [0.1011,0.4555]


Show code
grid(col = "grey90")





Beta(6,16) posterior with 95% equal-tail interval (blue) and HPD interval (red). Equal-tail is symmetric; HPD follows the peak and is slightly narrower.




The equal-tail interval is \([0.1368, 0.3846]\); the HPD is \([0.1295, 0.3791]\). The HPD is slightly narrower and excludes the extreme low-density tail on the left.

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.8 Posterior predictive distribution
Having estimated \(\theta\), we often want to predict a future observation \(x_{\text{new}}\).

The posterior predictive distribution integrates over parameter uncertainty: \[
p(x_{\text{new}} \mid D) = \int p(x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \, p(\theta \mid D) \, d\theta.
\] This is the probability distribution for a new observation, having learned \(\theta\) from data \(D\). No point estimate is needed; we marginalize over the posterior.


6.8.1 Prediction vs plug-in estimation
A naive approach is to plug in the point estimate \(\hat{\theta}\) (e.g., the posterior mean): \[
p_{\text{plug-in}}(x_{\text{new}} \mid D) \approx p(x_{\text{new}} \mid \hat{\theta}).
\]
This ignores parameter uncertainty, which can be substantial with sparse data.


6.8.2 Worked example: Poisson rate with zero counts
Suppose we observe zero events in \(n = 5\) time intervals: \(x_1 = \cdots = x_5 = 0\), so \(y = \sum_i x_i = 0\).
With a uniform prior \(\text{Gamma}(1, 0)\) (improper), the posterior is \(\lambda \mid D \sim \text{Gamma}(1, 5)\).
Plug-in estimate: \(\hat{\lambda}_{\text{MLE}} = y / n = 0\), so \(p(x_{\text{new}} = 1 \mid \hat{\lambda}) = 0\). This is unphysical: zero observed events do not mean events are impossible!
Posterior predictive: Integrating over the posterior, \[
p(x_{\text{new}} = 1 \mid D) = \int_0^\infty \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda}}{1!} \cdot 5e^{-5\lambda} d\lambda = \int_0^\infty 5\lambda e^{-6\lambda} d\lambda = \frac{5}{36} \approx 0.139.
\]
Even with zero observed counts, the posterior predicts a 14% chance of seeing an event in the next interval — a reasonable and physically sensible conclusion.


6.8.3 Variance decomposition
The predictive variance splits naturally into two sources: \[
\text{Var}(x_{\text{new}} \mid D) = E_\theta[\text{Var}(x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \mid D] + \text{Var}_\theta(E[x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta] \mid D).
\] The first term is data noise (aleatoric uncertainty); the second is parameter uncertainty (epistemic uncertainty). The full predictive captures both.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.9 Sequential learning and posterior updating
A key advantage of Bayesian inference is that learning is sequential: today’s posterior becomes tomorrow’s prior.

Sequential coin tosses
Start with a Beta(1,1) uniform prior. After observing data, we compute the posterior. If we observe more data, the posterior becomes the new prior.


Show code
set.seed(42)
p_true <- 0.7
n_obs_per_step <- 3
n_steps <- 4

par(mfrow = c(n_steps, 1), mar = c(3, 3, 2, 1), cex.main = 0.85)

p_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 1000)

# Initial prior
alpha <- 1
beta <- 1
prior_dens <- dbeta(p_grid, alpha, beta)

plot(p_grid, prior_dens, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#1f77b4",
     main = "Prior: Beta(1,1)", xlab = expression(p), ylab = "Density",
     ylim = c(0, 4))
abline(v = p_true, col = "#d62728", lwd = 1, lty = 2, alpha = 0.7)


Warning in int_abline(a = a, b = b, h = h, v = v, untf = untf, ...): "alpha" is
not a graphical parameter


Show code
grid(col = "grey90")

# Sequential updates
for (step in 1:n_steps) {
  # Generate new observation
  obs <- rbinom(n_obs_per_step, 1, p_true)
  k <- sum(obs)
  n <- n_obs_per_step

  # Update posterior (which becomes new prior)
  alpha <- alpha + k
  beta <- beta + n - k
  posterior_dens <- dbeta(p_grid, alpha, beta)

  # Plot
  plot(p_grid, posterior_dens, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#1f77b4",
       main = paste0("After ", step * n_obs_per_step, " observations"),
       xlab = expression(p), ylab = "Density", ylim = c(0, 4))
  abline(v = p_true, col = "#d62728", lwd = 1, lty = 2, alpha = 0.7)
  grid(col = "grey90")
}

text:
6.10 Frequentist confidence intervals: brief contrast
To highlight what is unique about Bayesian credible intervals, we briefly compare with frequentist confidence intervals.

A frequentist confidence interval is a random interval \(C(X)\) with coverage probability \(1 - \alpha\): \[
P(\theta \in C(X)) = 1 - \alpha,
\] where the probability is over repeated sampling of data \(X \sim p(x \mid \theta)\) under a fixed but unknown \(\theta\).
Interpretation: In repeated experiments, \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) of the confidence intervals will contain the true \(\theta\).


Key differences:








Aspect
Bayesian Credible Interval
Frequentist Confidence Interval




Probability on
Parameter
Data


Conditioned on
Observed data \(D\)
Hypothetical repeated datasets


Interpretation
\(P(\theta \in [a,b] \mid D) = \gamma\)
In repeated experiments, \(100\gamma\%\) of intervals cover \(\theta\)


Reparametrization
Invariant
Not invariant



Once we observe data, the frequentist interval either contains \(\theta\) or it does not — there is no probability statement about the observed interval.

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.11 One-sided credible intervals
For some problems, we care only about an upper or lower bound on the parameter. A one-sided credible interval \([0, \theta^*]\) at level \(\gamma\) satisfies \[
P(\theta \leq \theta^* \mid D) = \gamma.
\]

6.11.1 Rule of Three
A famous result in statistics applies to rare-event detection. If you observe zero events in \(n\) trials under a Binomial model with unknown rate \(p\), the 95% upper credible bound (using a uniform Beta(1,1) prior) is approximately \[
p^* \approx \frac{3}{n}.
\]
This is the “Rule of Three”: if you see zero occurrences in \(n\) trials, you can be 95% confident that the true rate is below \(3/n\).

Zero-count drug safety
If a drug is tested on 100 patients and zero adverse events are observed, the Rule of Three gives a 95% upper bound of \(3/100 = 0.03\) or 3%. We can be reasonably confident (in the Bayesian sense, given a weak prior) that the true adverse event rate is below 3%, even though we observed none.
This addresses the zero-count problem: the MLE would say the rate is exactly zero, which is overconfident. The Bayesian posterior hedges appropriately.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.12 Choosing priors: conjugacy and regularization

A prior \(\pi(\theta)\) is conjugate to a likelihood \(p(x \mid \theta)\) if the posterior is in the same family as the prior: \[
p(\theta \mid x) \propto p(x \mid \theta) \pi(\theta) \in \text{same family as } \pi(\theta).
\]

Conjugate priors are analytically tractable and allow simple sequential updating. They also naturally encode prior beliefs as “pseudo-data” or “pseudo-observations.”

6.12.1 Example: Beta-Binomial conjugacy

Prior: \(p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\) with density \(\pi(p) \propto p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}\).
Likelihood: \(P(k \text{ heads} \mid p, n) \propto p^k (1-p)^{n-k}\).
Posterior: \(p \mid k, n \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha + k, \beta + n - k)\).

The hyperparameters have an intuitive interpretation: \(\alpha - 1\) and \(\beta - 1\) are pseudo-successes and pseudo-failures, representing prior pseudo-data.


6.12.2 Example: Gamma-Poisson conjugacy

Prior: \(\lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with density \(\pi(\lambda) \propto \lambda^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta \lambda}\).
Likelihood: \(P(y \text{ counts} \mid \lambda, n) \propto \lambda^y e^{-n\lambda}\).
Posterior: \(\lambda \mid y, n \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha + y, \beta + n)\).

Here, \(\alpha\) is a pseudo-count and \(\beta\) is a pseudo-exposure time.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
section: 6.13 Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, and the Jeffreys prior
text:
6.13 Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, and the Jeffreys prior
The theory of parametric estimation rests on a few fundamental objects: the Fisher information matrix, which quantifies the curvature of the likelihood; the Cramér–Rao bound, which sets a lower limit on estimator variance; and the Jeffreys prior, which adapts to the geometry of the parameter space. This section develops these concepts in general, showing how they unify point estimation (Cramér–Rao), prior specification (Jeffreys), and asymptotic posterior behaviour (Bernstein–von Mises).

6.13.1 Score and Fisher Information

Given a model \(p(x \mid \theta)\) and an observation \(x\) (or dataset), the score vector is \[s(\theta; x) = \nabla_\theta \log p(x \mid \theta) = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \log p(x \mid \theta).\]
The Fisher information matrix is the expected outer product of the score: \[\mathcal{I}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_x\big[s(\theta;x)\,s(\theta;x)^{\top}\big],\] with \((i,j)\)-entry \(\mathcal{I}_{ij}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[s_i(\theta;x) s_j(\theta;x)]\).
Equivalently, under regularity conditions, \[\mathcal{I}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_x\left[\nabla_\theta^2 \log p(x\mid\theta)\right] = -\mathbb{E}[H(\theta)],\] where \(H(\theta)\) is the Hessian of the log-likelihood.

Proof sketch for the equivalence: Differentiating \(\int p(x\mid\theta) dx = 1\) with respect to \(\theta\): \[\int \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta} p(x\mid\theta) dx = \int p(x\mid\theta) \cdot s(\theta;x) dx = \mathbb{E}[s(\theta;x)] = 0.\] Differentiating again and swapping derivatives and integration (regularity): \[\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\partial s(\theta;x)}{\partial\theta}\right] + \mathbb{E}[s(\theta;x)^2] = 0,\] which gives \(\mathbb{E}[s(\theta;x)^2] = -\mathbb{E}[H(\theta)]\).
For an i.i.d. sample of size \(n\), the total score is the sum of individual scores, and the Fisher information scales linearly: \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta) = n\,\mathcal{I}(\theta)\).


6.13.2 Reparametrization Formula

Suppose we reparameterize via a smooth bijection \(\psi = \phi(\theta)\) with Jacobian matrix \(J_{ij} = \partial\psi_i / \partial\theta_j\). Then the Fisher information with respect to \(\psi\) is \[\mathcal{I}_\psi(\psi) = J^\top \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta) \, J,\] where \(J = \partial\phi(\theta) / \partial\theta\) evaluated at the appropriate \(\theta = \phi^{-1}(\psi)\).

Proof: By the chain rule, \(s_\psi(\psi; x) = J^\top s_\theta(\theta; x)\). Hence, \[\mathcal{I}_\psi(\psi) = \mathbb{E}[J^\top s_\theta s_\theta^\top J] = J^\top \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta) J.\]
This reparametrization formula is the key to understanding why \(\sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}(\theta)}\) behaves like an invariant density element. Under a change of variables \(\psi = \phi(\theta)\), \[\sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}_\psi(\psi)} = \sqrt{\det(J^\top J)} \cdot \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta)} = |\det J| \cdot \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta)},\] which is precisely the Jacobian rule for transforming a density. This geometric insight motivates the Jeffreys prior.


6.13.3 Cramér–Rao Lower Bound

Let \(\hat\theta(X)\) be an unbiased estimator of \(\theta\) from an i.i.d. sample \(X = (X_1, \ldots, X_n)\). Then \[\mathrm{Cov}(\hat\theta) \succeq \mathcal{I}_n(\theta)^{-1} = \frac{1}{n} \mathcal{I}(\theta)^{-1},\] where \(\succeq\) denotes the positive semidefinite order (i.e., \(\mathrm{Cov}(\hat\theta) - \mathcal{I}_n(\theta)^{-1}\) is positive semidefinite).

Proof outline (scalar case): Write the unbiasedness condition as \[\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta - \theta] = 0.\] Differentiating with respect to \(\theta\) and invoking regularity (swapping derivative and expectation): \[\mathbb{E}_\theta\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}(\hat\theta - \theta) \right] + \mathbb{E}_\theta[s(\theta;X)] = 0.\] Since \(\mathbb{E}[s] = 0\), we have \[\mathbb{E}[s(\theta;X)] = -1.\] Wait, I need to be more careful; let me re-phrase. Consider \(\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta \cdot s(\theta)] = \theta \mathbb{E}[s(\theta)]\). Differentiating the unbiasedness condition \(\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] = \theta\): \[\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\partial \hat\theta}{\partial\theta}\right] = 1 \quad \text{(in scalar case)}.\] By Cauchy–Schwarz applied to \(\hat\theta \cdot s\) and rearranging, one recovers \(\mathrm{Var}(\hat\theta) \ge \mathcal{I}(θ)^{-1}\).
The Cramér–Rao bound sets a fundamental speed limit on estimation: no unbiased estimator can have variance smaller than this bound. Remarkably, the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) achieves this bound asymptotically.


6.13.4 Asymptotic Normality of the MLE

Under standard regularity conditions, the MLE \(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{ML}}\) of \(\theta\) from a sample of size \(n\) satisfies \[\sqrt{n}\,(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{ML}} - \theta_*) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}\big(0,\,\mathcal{I}(\theta_*)^{-1}\big),\] where \(\theta_*\) is the true parameter and \(\mathcal{I}(\theta_*)\) is the Fisher information at the truth.

This result, whose proof relies on Tayor expansions of the score and regularity conditions, shows that for large \(n\) the MLE is approximately \(\mathcal{N}(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{ML}}, \mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*)^{-1})\). Since \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*) = n \mathcal{I}(\theta_*)\), the variance shrinks as \(1/n\), asymptotically achieving the Cramér–Rao bound. For reference on the detailed conditions and proof, see van der Vaart (1998), Chapter 5.


6.13.5 The Jeffreys Prior

The Jeffreys prior is defined as \[p_J(\theta) \propto \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}(\theta)},\] the square root of the determinant of the Fisher information matrix.

Intuitively, the Jeffreys prior allocates more prior mass to regions where the likelihood is less informative (where \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)\) is smaller) and less prior mass where the likelihood is highly peaked (where \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)\) is large). This gives an objective prior that “lets the data speak” in a geometric sense.


6.13.6 Reparametrisation Invariance of Jeffreys

If \(p_J(\theta) \propto \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta)}\) is the Jeffreys prior in the \(\theta\) parameterization, and we reparametrize via \(\psi = \phi(\theta)\) with Jacobian \(J\), then the Jeffreys prior in the \(\psi\) parameterization satisfies \[p_J(\psi) \propto \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}_\psi(\psi)} = \sqrt{\det(J^\top J)} \cdot \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta)} = |\det J| \cdot \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta)}.\]
Under the change of variables, the prior density transforms as \[p_J(\psi) \, d\psi = p_J(\theta)\,|\det J| \, d\theta,\] which shows that the Jeffreys prior is invariant under reparametrisation.

Significance: This is the defining property that distinguishes Jeffreys from a flat prior. A uniform prior \(p(\theta) \propto 1\) does not remain uniform after reparametrization; its density changes by the Jacobian. In contrast, the Jeffreys prior automatically adapts its form to remain “flat” in an information-geometric sense, independent of how we parameterize the model.


6.13.7 Examples of Jeffreys Priors

For a Bernoulli(\(p\)) model with a single observation \(x \in \{0,1\}\), the log-likelihood is \(x \log p + (1-x) \log(1-p)\). The Fisher information is \[\mathcal{I}(p) = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial p} \log p(x \mid p)\right)^2\right] = \frac{1}{p(1-p)}.\]
The Jeffreys prior is thus \[p_J(p) \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{p(1-p)}} = p^{-1/2}(1-p)^{-1/2},\] which is the Beta\((1/2, 1/2)\) distribution.
Note: the uniform prior is Beta\((1,1)\), which depends on the choice of the \(p\) parameterization. If we instead parameterize via the log-odds \(\phi = \log(p/(1-p))\), the uniform prior in \(\phi\) is different from the reparametrized form of Beta\((1,1)\). But Beta\((1/2, 1/2)\) remains the same (up to the appropriate density form) under any reparametrization.


For Poisson(\(\lambda\)), the log-likelihood is \(x \log \lambda - \lambda\), and the Fisher information is \[\mathcal{I}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{\lambda}.\]
The Jeffreys prior is \[p_J(\lambda) \propto \lambda^{-1/2},\] an improper prior. However, any observed data (\(x > 0\)) yields a proper posterior, because the likelihood \(\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}\) combined with \(\lambda^{-1/2}\) gives \(\lambda^{x-1/2} e^{-\lambda}\), a Gamma distribution.


With \(\sigma^2\) known, the log-likelihood for the mean \(\mu\) is proportional to \(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(x - \mu)^2\). The Fisher information is \[\mathcal{I}(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2},\] a positive constant independent of \(\mu\). The Jeffreys prior is thus \[p_J(\mu) \propto 1,\] a uniform (improper) prior—the standard choice for the unknown mean when the scale is known.


With \(\mu\) known, the log-likelihood is \(-\frac{n}{2}\log\sigma^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum(x - \mu)^2\). Differentiating twice with respect to \(\sigma^2\) gives \[\mathcal{I}(\sigma^2) = \frac{n}{2(\sigma^2)^2}.\]
The Jeffreys prior is \[p_J(\sigma^2) \propto \frac{1}{\sigma^2},\] or equivalently, \(p_J(\log \sigma) \propto 1\) (uniform on the log scale). This is the scale-invariant prior for variance, classical in Bayesian inference.


For the Gaussian with both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) unknown, the Fisher information matrix is \[\mathcal{I}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sigma^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2/\sigma^4 \end{pmatrix},\] so \(\det \mathcal{I} = 2/\sigma^6\), and the Jeffreys prior is \[p_J(\mu, \sigma^2) \propto \sqrt{\frac{2}{\sigma^6}} = \frac{1}{\sigma^3}.\]
Equivalently, \(p_J(\mu, \sigma) \propto 1/\sigma^2\) in terms of the standard deviation.
Subtle point: The joint Jeffreys prior \(p_J(\mu, \sigma^2) \propto 1/\sigma^3\) does not equal the product of the marginal Jeffreys priors for \(\mu\) (uniform) and \(\sigma^2\) (proportional to \(1/\sigma^2\)), which would give \(p(\mu) \cdot p(\sigma^2) \propto 1/\sigma^2\). Many practitioners use this product-of-marginals prior and call it “Jeffreys,” but it is technically the reference prior or a compromise prior. The true joint Jeffreys prior is \(1/\sigma^3\). This distinction matters for hypothesis tests and Bayes factors.



6.13.8 Reference Priors

Remark. When the parameter dimension is high, computing or integrating over the Jeffreys prior becomes cumbersome. Reference priors (Bernardo, 1979; Berger, Bernardo & Sun, 2009) generalize the idea: they maximize expected Kullback–Leibler divergence between posterior and prior, thus minimizing the information contained in the prior itself. For one-parameter problems, reference priors coincide with Jeffreys; for multiparameter problems, they offer an alternative construction that often performs better in frequentist coverage. Reference priors are not invariant to reparametrization but are designed to be more “objective” in the sense of information loss.



6.13.9 The Bernstein–von Mises Theorem

Under standard regularity conditions, for large sample size \(n\), the posterior distribution \(p(\theta \mid X_1, \ldots, X_n)\) is approximately Gaussian, centered at the MLE \(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{ML}}\) with covariance given by the inverse Fisher information: \[p(\theta \mid X_1, \ldots, X_n) \approx \mathcal{N}\big(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{ML}},\,\mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*)^{-1}\big),\] provided the prior is continuous and positive at \(\theta_*\) (the true parameter). This holds independently of the choice of prior.

Interpretation and significance: The Bernstein–von Mises theorem is one of the deepest results in Bayesian asymptotics. It says that, given enough data, the posterior “forgets” the prior (as long as it is not zero at the truth) and concentrates around the MLE with the canonical frequentist covariance \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*)^{-1}\). This explains why, asymptotically, Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist confidence intervals coincide: both converge to the same Gaussian limit. In other words, there is no fundamental conflict between Bayesian and frequentist inference in the large-sample regime—they merge. For a detailed proof, see van der Vaart (1998), Chapter 10.


6.13.10 Unifying Theme
Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, Jeffreys, and Bernstein–von Mises are tightly interwoven:

Cramér–Rao sets the performance standard for any unbiased estimator.
MLE asymptotics show that the MLE achieves this standard with variance \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta)^{-1}\).
Jeffreys prior is constructed from \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)\), ensuring objective prior choice via reparametrization invariance.
Bernstein–von Mises guarantees that the posterior, regardless of the prior, concentrates at the MLE with covariance \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta)^{-1}\).

Together, these results form the backbone of both frequentist and Bayesian asymptotic theory.

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.1 Introduction
The Gaussian (or normal) distribution is the most frequently encountered model in statistical inference. Its ubiquity stems from the Central Limit Theorem, which guarantees that sample averages tend toward normality under weak conditions, and from its mathematical tractability—the Gaussian family is closed under many operations and admits conjugate priors. This chapter develops both Bayesian and frequentist approaches to inference of the mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\) of a Gaussian model, emphasizing the conjugate prior structure that yields closed-form posterior distributions.
We begin with the simplest case: inference of the mean with known variance. We then extend to variance estimation with known mean, and finally treat joint inference of both parameters using the Normal-Inverse-Gamma conjugate family. Throughout, we highlight credible intervals, hypothesis testing via the Bayes factor, and the Bayesian \(t\)-test, which naturally marginalizes over the unknown variance.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
section: 7.2 Gaussian Sampling Model and Sufficient Statistics
text:
7.2 Gaussian Sampling Model and Sufficient Statistics

Let \(D = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}\) be \(n\) independent observations from a Gaussian distribution with pdf \[p(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

The likelihood function for the data is the product \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^n p(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2 \right).\]
Using the key identity \[\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2 + n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2 = S + n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2,\] where \(\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(S = \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2\), the likelihood becomes \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{S + n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

The statistics \(\bar{x}\) and \(S\) are sufficient for \((\mu, \sigma^2)\): they capture all information in the data relevant to parameter inference. Any inference on \((\mu, \sigma^2)\) depends on the data only through these two summaries.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
section: 7.3 Inference of the Mean with Known Variance
text:
7.3 Inference of the Mean with Known Variance
Assume \(\sigma^2\) is a known constant. The likelihood as a function of \(\mu\) is \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]
This is Gaussian in \(\mu\) centered at the sample mean \(\bar{x}\).

7.3.1 Uniform Prior and Posterior
Assign a uniform (improper) prior \(p(\mu) \propto 1\). This is also the Jeffreys prior for \(\mu\), since the Fisher information \(\mathcal{I}(\mu) = \frac{n}{\sigma^2}\) does not depend on \(\mu\).
By Bayes’ theorem, \[p(\mu \mid D, \sigma^2) \propto p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \cdot p(\mu) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

The posterior distribution of \(\mu\) is \[\mu \mid D, \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}\left( \bar{x}, \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \right).\]

The posterior mean equals the maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) \(\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x}\), and the posterior variance shrinks as \(1/n\), reflecting increased confidence with larger sample size.


7.3.2 Conjugate Normal-Normal Model
To incorporate prior knowledge, use a Gaussian prior \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)\).
The posterior is obtained by multiplying the likelihood and prior: \[p(\mu \mid D, \sigma^2) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right) \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right).\]
Completing the square in the exponent: \[p(\mu \mid D, \sigma^2) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{\kappa_n (\mu - \mu_n)^2}{2} \right),\] where the posterior precision and mean are

Normal-Normal Conjugate Update: \[\begin{align}
\kappa_n &= \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma^2}, \\
\mu_n &= \frac{\mu_0/\sigma_0^2 + n\bar{x}/\sigma^2}{\kappa_n} = \frac{\mu_0/\sigma_0^2 + n\bar{x}/\sigma^2}{1/\sigma_0^2 + n/\sigma^2}.
\end{align}\]

The posterior is \(\mu \mid D, \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_n, \kappa_n^{-1})\).
Interpretation: Precisions add; the posterior mean is a precision-weighted average of the prior mean and sample mean. As \(n \to \infty\), the posterior is dominated by the data (\(\mu_n \to \bar{x}\)). For a weak prior (\(\sigma_0^2 \to \infty\)), \(\kappa_n \to n/\sigma^2\) and \(\mu_n \to \bar{x}\), recovering the uniform prior result.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
section: 7.4 Inference of the Variance with Known Mean
text:
7.4 Inference of the Variance with Known Mean
Now assume \(\mu\) is known and infer \(\sigma^2\). The likelihood is \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{\sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]
Setting \(\nu = \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2\), the likelihood depends on \(\sigma^2\) via \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{\nu}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

7.4.1 Jeffreys Prior for Scale Parameters
Variance is a scale parameter: if we rescale \(x \to ax\), then \(\sigma^2 \to a^2\sigma^2\). As shown in Chapter 5 (§ Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, and the Jeffreys prior), the Jeffreys prior \(p_J(\theta) \propto \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}(\theta)}\) is reparametrization-invariant. For the Gaussian scale parameter \(\sigma^2\) with known mean, the Fisher information is \(\mathcal{I}(\sigma^2) = \frac{n}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\), yielding \(p_J(\sigma^2) \propto \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\) (or equivalently, \(p_J(\log\sigma^2) \propto 1\), uniform on log-scale). This prior ensures invariance under rescaling—critical for Bayes factors. Chapter 5 provides an extended example and reparametrization invariance proof; here we apply it directly to the Gaussian posterior.


7.4.2 Inverse-Gamma Conjugate Family
The Inverse-Gamma distribution is conjugate for variance estimation:

If \(X \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with pdf \(f(x) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta x}\), then \(1/X \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with pdf \[f(z) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} (1/z)^{\alpha+1} \exp(-\beta/z).\]

A prior \(\sigma^2 \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)\) combined with the likelihood yields a posterior:

Inverse-Gamma Conjugate Update: \[\sigma^2 \mid D, \mu \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}\left( \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2}, \beta_0 + \frac{\nu}{2} \right),\] where \(\nu = \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2\).

The Jeffreys prior corresponds to \(\alpha_0 = 0\) and \(\beta_0 = 0\) (or \(\alpha_0 = -1/2\) to ensure integrability). The posterior mean is \(\mathbb{E}[\sigma^2 \mid D] = \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n - 1}\) for \(\alpha_n > 1\).
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.5 Joint Inference: Normal-Inverse-Gamma Model
Most realistic: both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are unknown. Use a factorized prior \[p(\mu, \sigma^2) = p(\mu \mid \sigma^2) p(\sigma^2),\] with \(\mu \mid \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma^2/\kappa_0)\) and \(\sigma^2 \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)\).
This is the Normal-Inverse-Gamma (NIG) conjugate family with hyperparameters \((\mu_0, \kappa_0, \alpha_0, \beta_0)\).

7.5.1 Posterior Update
Combining likelihood with the NIG prior yields a posterior with hyperparameters:

Normal-Inverse-Gamma Conjugate Update: \[\begin{align}
\kappa_n &= \kappa_0 + n, \\
\mu_n &= \frac{\kappa_0 \mu_0 + n\bar{x}}{\kappa_n}, \\
\alpha_n &= \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2}, \\
\beta_n &= \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2} + \frac{\kappa_0 n}{2(\kappa_0 + n)} (\bar{x} - \mu_0)^2,
\end{align}\] where \(S = \sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2\).

The joint posterior factorizes as \[p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid D) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-(\alpha_n + 1)} \exp\left( -\frac{\beta_n}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\kappa_n(\mu - \mu_n)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]


7.5.2 Marginal Posterior of the Mean
Marginalizing \(\sigma^2\) yields the marginal posterior of \(\mu\):

The posterior marginal distribution of \(\mu\) given \(D\) is \[\mu \mid D \sim t_{2\alpha_n}\left( \mu_n, \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n \kappa_n} \right),\] a Student-\(t\) distribution with \(\nu = 2\alpha_n\) degrees of freedom, location \(\mu_n\), and scale parameter \(\sqrt{\beta_n/(\alpha_n \kappa_n)}\).

Interpretation: The heavy tails of the \(t\)-distribution reflect additional uncertainty from not knowing \(\sigma^2\). For large \(n\), \(\nu = 2\alpha_n \approx n \to \infty\), so \(t\)-distribution converges to Gaussian, recovering asymptotic normality.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.6 Credible Intervals and Regions

7.6.1 Central Credible Interval for the Mean
A central \((1-\alpha)\)% credible interval is \[\mu \pm t_{2\alpha_n, 1-\alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n \kappa_n}},\] where \(t_{\nu, p}\) denotes the \(p\)-quantile of a Student-\(t\) distribution with \(\nu\) degrees of freedom.
Frequentist analogue: In the large-sample limit, this recovers the familiar confidence interval \(\hat{\mu} \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2/n}\).


7.6.2 Joint Credible Region
A joint \((1-\alpha)\)% credible region for \(\theta = (\mu, \sigma^2)\) is the ellipse \[(\theta - \hat{\theta})^T \mathcal{I}_n(\hat{\theta}) (\theta - \hat{\theta}) \le \chi^2_{2, 1-\alpha},\] where \(\mathcal{I}_n(\hat{\theta})\) is the Fisher information matrix evaluated at the MLE and \(\chi^2_{2, 1-\alpha}\) is the \((1-\alpha)\) quantile of the chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom.
The posterior asymptotically factorizes: the estimated correlation between \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) vanishes as \(n \to \infty\). This convergence to Gaussian form and factorization is guaranteed by the Bernstein–von Mises theorem (Chapter 5, § Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, and the Jeffreys prior), which states that the posterior becomes approximately \(\mathcal{N}(\hat{\theta}_{\mathrm{ML}}, \mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*)^{-1})\) for large \(n\), independently of the prior. This is why Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist confidence intervals asymptotically agree.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.7 Bayesian Hypothesis Testing

7.7.1 One-Sided Test for the Mean
Consider \(H_0: \mu \le \mu^* \text{ vs. } H_1: \mu > \mu^*\).
The posterior probability of \(H_1\) is \[P(H_1 \mid D) = 1 - F_t\left( \frac{\mu^* - \mu_n}{\sqrt{\beta_n/(\alpha_n \kappa_n)}} \right),\] where \(F_t\) is the CDF of a Student-\(t\) distribution with \(2\alpha_n\) degrees of freedom.
A decision rule: reject \(H_0\) if \(P(H_1 \mid D) > 1 - \alpha\). Equivalently, reject if \(\mu^*\) lies below the lower bound of the \((1-\alpha)\)% credible interval.


7.7.2 Two-Sided Test and Bayes Factors
For \(H_0: \mu = \mu^* \text{ vs. } H_1: \mu \ne \mu^*\), compare the marginal likelihoods:

The Bayes factor of \(H_1\) over \(H_0\) is \[\text{BF}_{10} = \frac{p(D \mid H_1)}{p(D \mid H_0)} = \frac{\int p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid H_1) d\mu d\sigma^2}{\int_{\mu=\mu^*} p(D \mid \mu^*, \sigma^2) p(\sigma^2 \mid H_0) d\sigma^2}.\]

Interpretation: - \(\text{BF}_{10} > 1\): evidence favors \(H_1\) (mean differs from \(\mu^*\)). - \(\text{BF}_{10} < 1\): evidence favors \(H_0\). - Magnitude quantifies strength of evidence. The Bayes factor, unlike frequentist \(p\)-values, is invariant under the choice of scale (Jeffreys prior ensures this).


7.7.3 Bayesian \(t\)-Test
The Bayesian \(t\)-test compares observed \(t = (\bar{x} - \mu^*) / \sqrt{S/(n(n-1))}\) to its posterior predictive distribution under \(H_0\). With the NIG conjugate prior, the marginal likelihood under \(H_0\) is a \(t\)-distribution, naturally accounting for variance uncertainty without ad-hoc degrees-of-freedom corrections.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.8 Posterior Predictive Distribution
Given posterior \(p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid D)\), the posterior predictive distribution for a new observation \(\tilde{x}\) is \[p(\tilde{x} \mid D) = \int p(\tilde{x} \mid \mu, \sigma^2) p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid D) d\mu d\sigma^2.\]
For the NIG model, this marginalizes to a Student-\(t\) distribution with the same degrees of freedom and location as the posterior for \(\mu\), but with inflated scale (reflecting both parameter and observational uncertainty): \[\tilde{x} \mid D \sim t_{2\alpha_n}\left( \mu_n, \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n} \left(1 + \frac{1}{\kappa_n}\right) \right).\]
The posterior predictive intervals are wider than credible intervals for the mean, appropriately quantifying full predictive uncertainty.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.9 Frequentist Contrast
The frequentist approach uses the sample \(t\)-statistic \[t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sqrt{S / (n(n-1))}},\] which under \(H_0: \mu = \mu^*\) follows \(t_{n-1}\). Rejection is based on \(p\)-value: \(p = P(|T| \ge |t_{\text{obs}}| \mid H_0)\).
Key differences from Bayes:

Frequentist \(t\)-test: assumes \(\mu^*\) exactly true under \(H_0\); \(p\)-value is the probability of observing data as extreme or more extreme under repeated sampling.
Bayesian \(t\)-test: posterior probability that \(\mu > \mu^*\) (or any specified region); updates beliefs given observed data; allows flexible loss functions for decision-making.
Sample size: Frequentist \(p\)-values can remain small for minor effects with large \(n\). Bayes factors adapt: as data accumulate under \(H_1\), they grow; but if data weakly support \(H_1\) relative to prediction, BF remains modest.
Asymptotic equivalence: For fixed effect size and large \(n\), frequentist confidence intervals and Bayesian credible intervals converge; the posterior becomes approximately Gaussian by Bernstein–von Mises.
