title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.1 Introduction: The multimodality trap
Standard Metropolis–Hastings and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo samplers work well when the target posterior is unimodal or has modes close together. However, when the posterior has several well-separated modes, a single MCMC chain becomes trapped near the mode it encounters first. It cannot escape because crossing the energy barrier between modes occurs with probability decaying exponentially in the barrier height.
Consider two modes separated by a barrier of height \(\Delta E\). The probability of escaping is approximately \[p \approx \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)\] by the Arrhenius formula, where we write the posterior as \(\pi(\theta) = \exp[-E(\theta)]\). When \(\Delta E \gg 1\), this probability is negligibly small, and the chain explores only a tiny fraction of the parameter space.
Simulated annealing and parallel tempering both tackle this problem by introducing a temperature parameter. They exploit a key observation: at high temperature \(T\), the posterior is flattened, and energy barriers become easier to cross. The two methods differ in how they use this idea.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.2 Tempered distributions and temperatures

11.2.1 Definition
For any target posterior \(\pi(\theta)\) with energy representation \(\pi(\theta) = \exp[-E(\theta)]\), we define the tempered posterior at inverse temperature \(\beta \in (0, 1]\) by \[\pi_\beta(\theta) \propto \pi(\theta)^\beta = \exp[-\beta E(\theta)].\]
Equivalently, using temperature \(T = 1/\beta\), \[\pi_T(\theta) \propto \pi(\theta)^{1/T} = \exp\left[-\frac{E(\theta)}{T}\right].\]


11.2.2 Limiting behaviour

Cold limit (\(\beta \to 1\) or \(T \to 1\)): \(\pi_\beta(\theta) \to \pi(\theta)\), the original posterior.
Hot limit (\(\beta \to 0\) or \(T \to \infty\)): \(\pi_\beta(\theta)\) becomes nearly flat, and the chain explores the entire space freely.
Concentrated limits (\(\beta \to 0\) in the context of optimization): As the temperature decreases to zero, \(\pi_T(\theta)\) concentrates its mass on the global minima of \(E(\theta)\).

At high temperature, the contrast between modes vanishes, making it easy for the chain to move between them. As the temperature decreases, the modes become increasingly separated again.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.3 Simulated annealing

11.3.1 Purpose and algorithm
Simulated annealing is an optimization method that cools a single chain to find the maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimator \(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta \pi(\theta)\). It generates a sequence of increasingly concentrated distributions by running a cooling schedule.
Simulated annealing algorithm: 1. Initialize \(\theta_0\) and temperature \(T_0 > 0\). 2. For \(k = 0, 1, \ldots, K\): - Propose \(\theta' \sim q(\theta' \mid \theta_k)\). - Accept with probability \(\alpha_T(\theta_k \to \theta') = \min\{1, \exp[-\beta_k(E(\theta') - E(\theta_k))]\}\) where \(\beta_k = 1/T_k\). - Update \(T_{k+1} = \gamma T_k\) for some \(0 < \gamma < 1\) (e.g., geometric cooling) or use \(T_k = c / \log k\) (logarithmic cooling). 3. Return the state \(\theta\) with highest \(\pi(\theta)\) encountered.


11.3.2 Cooling schedule and barrier crossing
A crucial result: if we use a logarithmic cooling schedule \(T_k = c / \log k\), then the chain crosses any fixed energy barrier infinitely often (with probability 1) as \(k \to \infty\). This is because the cumulative escape probability \[\sum_{k=1}^\infty p_k = \sum_{k=1}^\infty \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta E \cdot \log k}{c}\right) = \sum_{k=1}^\infty k^{-\Delta E/c}\] diverges if and only if \(\Delta E/c \leq 1\), i.e., \(c \geq \Delta E\). Thus simulated annealing is theoretically guaranteed to find the global optimum (almost surely) with sufficiently slow cooling. In practice, slower cooling requires more iterations, and geometric schedules are often preferred for computational speed.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.4 Parallel tempering (replica exchange)

11.4.1 The parallel tempering framework
Parallel tempering runs \(K\) chains in parallel, each at a different inverse temperature \(\beta_1 = 1 > \beta_2 > \cdots > \beta_K > 0\). The chain at \(\beta_1 = 1\) is the “cold chain” that targets the posterior of interest. Chains at smaller \(\beta\) are “hot chains” that explore more freely.
The key innovation is swaps: occasionally, we exchange the states of neighbouring chains. This allows the cold chain to benefit from discoveries made by hot chains, enabling it to escape local modes without the cold chain ever needing to climb the barrier alone.


11.4.2 Joint target
Let \(\theta_l\) denote the state of chain \(l\). The joint state is \(\Theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_K)\), and the product target is \[\Pi(\Theta) = \prod_{l=1}^K \pi_{\beta_l}(\theta_l) \propto \prod_{l=1}^K \pi(\theta_l)^{\beta_l}.\]

The marginal distribution of \(\theta_1\) (the cold chain) is exactly \[\pi_{\beta_1}(\theta_1) = \pi(\theta_1),\] since \(\beta_1 = 1\). Samples from the cold chain are valid posterior samples, and expectations computed from the cold chain are unbiased estimates of posterior expectations.



11.4.3 Within-temperature moves
At each temperature, we perform a standard Metropolis–Hastings move with the tempered acceptance probability. For chain \(l\): \[\alpha_l(\theta_l \to \theta_l') = \min\left\{1, \left(\frac{\pi(\theta_l')}{\pi(\theta_l)}\right)^{\beta_l} \frac{q(\theta_l \mid \theta_l')}{q(\theta_l' \mid \theta_l)}\right\}.\]
Hot chains (small \(\beta_l\)) accept uphill moves more readily, allowing them to traverse the landscape freely.


11.4.4 Swap moves and detailed balance
The second type of move exchanges states between neighbouring chains \(l\) and \(l+1\): \[(\theta_l, \theta_{l+1}) \to (\theta_{l+1}, \theta_l).\]
The Metropolis acceptance probability for this move is \[\alpha_{\text{swap}}(\theta_l, \theta_{l+1}) = \min\left\{1, \frac{\pi(\theta_{l+1})^{\beta_l} \pi(\theta_l)^{\beta_{l+1}}}{\pi(\theta_l)^{\beta_l} \pi(\theta_{l+1})^{\beta_{l+1}}}\right\}.\]
In log form (preferred numerically): \[\log \alpha_{\text{swap}} = \min\{0, (\beta_l - \beta_{l+1})[E(\theta_l) - E(\theta_{l+1})]\}.\]
The normalizing constants cancel, making the swap probability independent of the partition function.

The combined algorithm of within-temperature MH updates and swap moves satisfies detailed balance with respect to the product target \(\Pi(\Theta)\). Therefore, the cold chain’s marginal converges to \(\pi\).


Proof. Within-temperature moves leave \(\pi_{\beta_l}(\theta_l)\) invariant at each temperature. Swap moves satisfy detailed balance for the product density: the probability of being in state \((\theta_l, \theta_{l+1})\) and swapping to \((\theta_{l+1}, \theta_l)\) equals the probability of being in \((\theta_{l+1}, \theta_l)\) and swapping back (by the Metropolis ratio). The product of these two reversible operations preserves \(\Pi(\Theta)\).



11.4.5 How parallel tempering works

Hot chains explore freely: Because \(\beta_l\) is small for hot chains, they traverse the landscape easily, finding new modes.
Swaps transport information: When a hot chain discovers a mode, a swap can move it to a colder chain, bringing the discovery closer to the target.
Cold chain remains on target: The cold chain never leaves the target distribution \(\pi\), yet it benefits from the hot chains’ discoveries via successful swaps.

This is the crucial difference from simulated annealing: the temperature is fixed, not decreasing. The algorithm is homogeneous, run indefinitely, and all cold-chain samples are valid posterior samples.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.5 Temperature ladder design

11.5.1 Geometric spacing
The spacing of temperatures is critical. If \(\beta\) values are too far apart, swap acceptance rates become vanishingly small, and information cannot flow between chains. If they are too close, many chains are needed.
A simple and effective choice is geometric spacing: \[\beta_l = \beta_1 r^{l-1}, \quad 0 < r < 1.\]
This keeps temperatures evenly spaced in a logarithmic sense.


11.5.2 Target swap acceptance
A practical rule of thumb is to set the temperature ladder such that the swap acceptance probability between adjacent chains is in the range 20–40%. This balances exploration and computational efficiency.


11.5.3 All-pairs vs. neighbour-only swaps
The basic algorithm swaps only neighbouring pairs. Allowing swaps between arbitrary chains (“all-pairs”) can improve mixing but at higher computational cost. For most problems, neighbour-only swaps are sufficient if the ladder is carefully tuned.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.6 Worked example: bimodal 1D mixture

Consider a bimodal posterior that is a mixture of two Gaussians: \[\pi(\theta) \propto 0.35 \, N(\theta; -4, 1) + 0.65 \, N(\theta; 3, 0.7^2).\]
The right mode is higher. We will show that a single-chain MH sampler gets trapped, while parallel tempering explores both modes.



Show code
# Log-posterior for bimodal mixture (minus normalization)
log_pi <- function(x) {
  a <- log(0.35) + dnorm(x, -4, 1, log = TRUE)
  b <- log(0.65) + dnorm(x, 3, 0.7, log = TRUE)
  m <- pmax(a, b)
  m + log(exp(a - m) + exp(b - m))
}

# Target density (for plotting)
pi_density <- function(x) {
  0.35 * dnorm(x, -4, 1) + 0.65 * dnorm(x, 3, 0.7)
}

# Single MH step at temperature beta
tempered_step <- function(x, beta, step = 1.0) {
  x_prop <- x + rnorm(1, 0, step)
  loga <- beta * (log_pi(x_prop) - log_pi(x))
  if (log(runif(1)) < loga) x_prop else x
}




Show code
single_chain_mh <- function(n_iter = 20000, x0 = -4, step = 1.0) {
  x <- x0
  out <- numeric(n_iter)
  for (i in 1:n_iter) {
    x <- tempered_step(x, beta = 1.0, step = step)
    out[i] <- x
  }
  out
}

set.seed(1)
trace_single <- single_chain_mh(n_iter = 20000, x0 = -4, step = 1.2)




Show code
parallel_tempering <- function(n_iter = 20000,
                               betas = c(1.0, 0.65, 0.4, 0.2, 0.08),
                               step = 1.2) {
  L <- length(betas)
  x <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = L)  # Initialize chains at different locations

  x_by_temp <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_iter, ncol = L)

  for (i in 1:n_iter) {
    # Within-temperature MH updates
    for (l in 1:L) {
      x[l] <- tempered_step(x[l], betas[l], step = step)
    }

    # Swap moves between neighbouring chains
    for (l in sample(1:(L - 1))) {
      loga <- (betas[l] - betas[l + 1]) * (log_pi(x[l + 1]) - log_pi(x[l]))
      if (log(runif(1)) < loga) {
        tmp <- x[l]
        x[l] <- x[l + 1]
        x[l + 1] <- tmp
      }
    }

    # Store state of cold chain (beta = 1)
    x_by_temp[i, ] <- x
  }

  list(x_by_temp = x_by_temp, betas = betas)
}

set.seed(1)
res_pt <- parallel_tempering(n_iter = 20000,
                             betas = c(1.0, 0.65, 0.4, 0.2, 0.08),
                             step = 1.2)




Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
plot(trace_single[1:5000], type = "l", lwd = 1.2,
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = expression(theta),
     main = "Single-chain MH trace (first 5000 iterations)",
     col = "black", ylim = c(-6, 4))
grid()








Figure 11.1: Single-chain MH on bimodal target: stays trapped near the left mode.







Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
cold_trace <- res_pt$x_by_temp[1:5000, 1]
plot(cold_trace, type = "l", lwd = 1.2,
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = expression(theta),
     main = "Parallel tempering: cold chain trace (first 5000 iterations)",
     col = "black", ylim = c(-6, 4))
grid()








Figure 11.2: Parallel tempering: cold chain (β=1) explores both modes via swaps.







Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))

# Burn-in
burn <- 5000
x_single_burn <- trace_single[-(1:burn)]
x_pt_burn <- res_pt$x_by_temp[-(1:burn), 1]

# Plot true density
xx <- seq(-7, 5, length.out = 200)
plot(xx, pi_density(xx), type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "black",
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = "Density",
     main = "Posterior: true density vs. samples",
     ylim = c(0, max(pi_density(xx)) * 1.1))

# Histogram of single-chain samples
hist(x_single_burn, breaks = 60, freq = FALSE, col = rgb(0.8, 0.8, 0.8, 0.5),
     border = "gray40", add = TRUE)

# Histogram of PT samples
hist(x_pt_burn, breaks = 60, freq = FALSE, col = rgb(0.7, 0.7, 0.7, 0.3),
     border = "black", add = TRUE)

legend("topright", bty = "n", cex = 0.75,
       legend = c("True density", "Single-chain MH samples", "PT cold chain samples"),
       lty = c(1, NA, NA), col = c("black", NA, NA),
       fill = c(NA, rgb(0.8, 0.8, 0.8, 0.5), rgb(0.7, 0.7, 0.7, 0.3)),
       border = c(NA, "gray40", "black"))








Figure 11.3: Posterior samples: single-chain MH misses the left mode, PT captures both.







Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))

betas_plot <- c(1.0, 0.65, 0.4, 0.15)
xx <- seq(-7, 5, length.out = 200)
colors_seq <- c("black", "gray40", "gray60", "gray70")
ltys <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)

plot(xx, pi_density(xx)^betas_plot[1], type = "l", lwd = 1.2, col = colors_seq[1],
     lty = ltys[1], xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(pi(theta)^beta),
     main = "Effect of inverse temperature on posterior shape",
     ylim = c(0, max(pi_density(xx)^betas_plot[1]) * 1.05))

for (i in 2:length(betas_plot)) {
  lines(xx, pi_density(xx)^betas_plot[i], lwd = 1.2, col = colors_seq[i], lty = ltys[i])
}

legend("topright", bty = "n", cex = 0.75,
       legend = paste0(expression(beta), " = ", betas_plot),
       col = colors_seq, lty = ltys, lwd = 1.2)








Figure 11.4: Tempered posteriors at different inverse temperatures, showing flattening at hot chains.





The figures above illustrate the fundamental problem and solution:

Figure 1 shows a single chain trapped in the left mode.
Figure 2 shows the parallel tempering cold chain (at \(\beta = 1\)) successfully switching between both modes.
Figure 3 shows the histogram of samples: single-chain MH misses the left mode entirely, while PT recovers the true bimodal structure.
Figure 4 visualizes how higher temperature (\(\beta \to 0\)) flattens the posterior, enabling free movement.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.7 Practical considerations

11.7.1 When to use parallel tempering
Use parallel tempering when: - The posterior is multimodal with well-separated modes. - Standard MCMC chains get trapped. - Interest is in accurate posterior sampling and uncertainty quantification. - The problem involves phase transitions or critical phenomena (common in physics).
Use simulated annealing when: - The goal is to find the MAP estimate or a point estimate. - Optimization (not sampling) is the primary objective. - You need fast identification of the best fit, not uncertainty.
Avoid parallel tempering (prefer HMC/NUTS) when: - The posterior is unimodal and high-dimensional. - Gradient information is available. - Computational resources are limited and you cannot afford many chains.
HMC and NUTS excel in high dimensions because they use gradient information to make large, informed moves. However, they fail on multimodal targets because a single trajectory cannot jump between modes.


11.7.2 Computational cost
Running \(K\) chains costs roughly \(K\) times as much as a single chain. However, this is often money well spent: the mixing time of the cold chain typically decreases dramatically when the temperature ladder is well-designed.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.8 Summary

Tempered distributions scale the exponent of the posterior: \(\pi_\beta(\theta) \propto \pi(\theta)^\beta\). At \(\beta = 1\) we recover the target; at \(\beta \to 0\) the distribution flattens.

Simulated annealing is a single-chain method with a time-varying cooling schedule; it targets optimization (MAP estimation) and asymptotically concentrates on the global maximum.
Parallel tempering (replica exchange MCMC) runs \(K\) chains at fixed inverse temperatures \(1 = \beta_1 > \beta_2 > \cdots > \beta_K\) and periodically swaps states between neighbours. The cold chain remains on the target distribution throughout and escapes local modes via information shared from hotter chains.
Detailed balance is satisfied: within-temperature moves are standard MH, and swap moves satisfy detailed balance with respect to the product target \(\Pi(\Theta) = \prod_{l=1}^K \pi_{\beta_l}(\theta_l)\). Thus the cold chain’s marginal is exactly \(\pi\).
Temperature ladder design is critical: geometric spacing in inverse temperature and target swap acceptance rates of 20–40% are practical guidelines.
The worked example shows that parallel tempering succeeds where single-chain MH fails on a bimodal target, recovering the true posterior over both modes.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.9 Sources

Falasco, G. Advanced Statistics for Physics Analysis: Monte Carlo methods and annealing for multimodal targets. Lecture slides, May 8, 2026.
Robert, C. P. and Casella, G. Monte Carlo Statistical Methods. Springer, 2nd ed., 2004. Chapter 10–11 (tempering, reversible jump, Gibbs sampling).
Geyer, C. J. (1991). Markov chain Monte Carlo maximum likelihood. In Proceedings of the 23rd Symposium on the Interface, Fairfax Station, VA.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.1 Introduction
Regression analysis seeks to learn a functional relationship between input variables \(x\) and observed response variables \(y\) from data. In the Bayesian framework, we construct a probabilistic model that combines a generative (noise-free) function \(f(x; \theta)\) with a noise model, then use Bayes’ theorem to infer the posterior distribution \(p(\theta \mid D)\) of the parameters. For regression models where the parameters enter linearly, exact posterior inference is tractable via conjugate analysis. For nonlinear models or non-Gaussian likelihoods, we rely on Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling. This chapter develops both the theory and practice of Bayesian regression: from the closed-form solution for linear Gaussian regression to the complete MCMC workflow for fitting arbitrary nonlinear regression models and making probabilistic predictions.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.2 Regression Model: Signal Plus Noise

12.2.1 Model Formulation
The basic regression model specifies that each observed response \(y_i\) arises from a noise-free mean function plus random noise:
\[y_i = f(x_i; \theta) + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2).\]
Here: - \(x_i\) is the regressor (input, explanatory variable), assumed fixed and measured without error. - \(y_i\) is the response (dependent, outcome variable). - \(f(x_i; \theta)\) is the signal—the deterministic part. - \(\varepsilon_i\) is measurement noise or intrinsic scatter, i.i.d. Gaussian with variance \(\sigma^2\).


12.2.2 Likelihood for Gaussian Noise
Under independent Gaussian noise, each observation has conditional distribution \[p(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = \mathcal{N}(f(x_i; \theta), \sigma^2).\]
For \(N\) independent observations \(D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N\), the joint likelihood factorizes: \[p(D \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^N p(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^N \mathcal{N}(f(x_i; \theta), \sigma^2).\]
Taking logarithms, \[\log p(D \mid \theta) = -N \log\sigma - \frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^N [y_i - f(x_i; \theta)]^2 + \text{const}.\]

title: 12  Bayesian regression
section: 12.3 Linear Models and the Design Matrix
text:
12.3 Linear Models and the Design Matrix

12.3.1 Linearity in Parameters
A regression model is linear if the parameters enter linearly, even if the features depend nonlinearly on the inputs:
\[y_i = \boldsymbol{\phi}(x_i)^\top \mathbf{w} + \varepsilon_i,\]
where \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = [\phi_1(x), \ldots, \phi_M(x)]^\top\) is a feature map and \(\mathbf{w} = [w_0, \ldots, w_{M-1}]^\top\) is the weight vector.

Examples of linear regression models: - Straight line: \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = (1, x)\), \(f(x) = w_0 + w_1 x\). - Polynomial: \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = (1, x, x^2, \ldots, x^{M-1})\), \(f(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{M-1} w_j x^j\). - Fourier: \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = (1, \sin(x), \cos(x), \ldots)\). - Linear in unknown parameters: If \(f(x) = w_0 e^{-\lambda x}\) with \(\lambda\) known, it is linear in \(w_0\). - Not linear in unknown parameters: \(f(x) = w_0 e^{-\lambda x}\) if \(\lambda\) is unknown (exponential, logistic, etc.).



12.3.2 Design Matrix Notation
For \(N\) data points and \(M\) basis functions, define the \(N \times M\) design matrix (or regressor matrix) \[\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix}
\phi_1(x_1) & \phi_2(x_1) & \cdots & \phi_M(x_1) \\
\phi_1(x_2) & \phi_2(x_2) & \cdots & \phi_M(x_2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\phi_1(x_N) & \phi_2(x_N) & \cdots & \phi_M(x_N)
\end{bmatrix}.\]
The linear model becomes \[\mathbf{y} = \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_N),\]
and the likelihood is \[p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_N).\]

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.4 Bayesian Linear Regression: Closed-Form Solution

12.4.1 Conjugate Prior and Posterior
Assume \(\sigma^2\) is known. Choose a Gaussian prior on \(\mathbf{w}\): \[p(\mathbf{w}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{m}_0, \mathbf{S}_0).\]
The likelihood is Gaussian in \(\mathbf{w}\); by conjugacy, the posterior is also Gaussian:

Bayesian Linear Regression with Gaussian Prior:
Given likelihood \(p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_N)\) and prior \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{m}_0, \mathbf{S}_0)\), the posterior is \[p(\mathbf{w} \mid D, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{m}_N, \mathbf{S}_N),\]
where \[\begin{align}
\mathbf{S}_N^{-1} &= \mathbf{S}_0^{-1} + \sigma^{-2} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \\
\mathbf{m}_N &= \mathbf{S}_N \left( \mathbf{S}_0^{-1} \mathbf{m}_0 + \sigma^{-2} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \mathbf{y} \right).
\end{align}\]

Interpretation: Precisions add. The posterior mean is a precision-weighted average of the prior mean and the data-driven estimate. The posterior covariance \(\mathbf{S}_N\) shrinks relative to \(\mathbf{S}_0\).


12.4.2 Special Case: Weak Isotropic Prior
A common choice is the weak prior \(\mathbf{m}_0 = \mathbf{0}\) and \(\mathbf{S}_0 = \sigma_0^2 \mathbf{I}_M\): \[\mathbf{S}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \mathbf{I}_M + \sigma^{-2} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \boldsymbol{\Phi},\] \[\mathbf{m}_N = \sigma^{-2} \mathbf{S}_N \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \mathbf{y}.\]


12.4.3 Connection to Ridge Regression
Ordinary least squares minimizes \(\|\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}\|^2\). Ridge regression adds an L2 penalty: \[L_{\text{ridge}} = \|\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}\|^2 + \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|^2.\]
The ridge estimator is \(\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{\text{ridge}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \boldsymbol{\Phi} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \mathbf{y}\).
Ridge regression is maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation under a Gaussian prior: the prior variance relates to the penalty by \(\lambda = \sigma^2 / \sigma_0^2\). The Bayesian approach goes further by quantifying posterior uncertainty via the full covariance \(\mathbf{S}_N\).

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.5 Posterior Predictive Distribution

12.5.1 Parameter Uncertainty and Prediction Uncertainty
For a new input $x_* $, we wish to predict \(y_*\). The posterior predictive distribution marginalizes over parameter uncertainty: \[p(y_* \mid x_*, D, \sigma^2) = \int p(y_* \mid x_*, \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2) \, p(\mathbf{w} \mid D, \sigma^2) \, d\mathbf{w}.\]
Since both terms are Gaussian, the integral is tractable:

Posterior Predictive Distribution:
Let \(\boldsymbol{\phi}_* = \boldsymbol{\phi}(x_*)\). Then \[p(y_* \mid x_*, D, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}\left( y_* \,\big|\, \boldsymbol{\phi}_*^\top \mathbf{m}_N, \, \sigma^2 + \boldsymbol{\phi}_*^\top \mathbf{S}_N \boldsymbol{\phi}_* \right).\]

Two sources of uncertainty in prediction: 1. Observation noise: \(\sigma^2\) (irreducible error in \(y_*\)). 2. Parameter uncertainty: \(\boldsymbol{\phi}_*^\top \mathbf{S}_N \boldsymbol{\phi}_*\) (uncertainty in the regression function \(f(x_*)\)).
The posterior predictive band widens away from the data due to increasing parameter uncertainty.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.6 Unknown Noise Level
In practice, \(\sigma^2\) is usually unknown and inferred from data. The parameters become \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{w}, \sigma)\), and we use a prior on the noise scale. A standard choice is the Jeffreys prior for scale parameters: \[p(\sigma) \propto \frac{1}{\sigma}, \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad p(\log \sigma) \text{ is uniform}.\]
The joint posterior is \[p(\mathbf{w}, \sigma \mid D) \propto p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \mathbf{w}, \sigma) \, p(\mathbf{w}) \, p(\sigma).\]
When both \(\mathbf{w}\) and \(\sigma\) are unknown, the posterior no longer has a closed form in closed-form distributions, but conjugate analysis can still apply to marginal posteriors or conditional distributions. For computational convenience and to make use of existing MCMC software, we often resort to sampling.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.7 Nonlinear Regression and MCMC

12.7.1 Why Nonlinear Models Require MCMC
A model is nonlinear in parameters if \(f(x; \theta)\) is a nonlinear function of some \(\theta\). Examples: - Exponential: \(f(x) = a e^{b x}\) (nonlinear in both \(a\) and \(b\) unless one is known). - Logistic: \(f(x) = \frac{L}{1 + e^{-k(x - x_0)}}\) (nonlinear in location \(x_0\), scale \(k\), amplitude \(L\)). - Power law: \(f(x) = a x^b\) (nonlinear in exponent \(b\)).
For such models, the likelihood is no longer Gaussian in the parameters, and conjugate priors do not yield closed-form posteriors. Similarly, generalized linear models (logistic regression for binary data, Poisson regression for counts) have non-Gaussian likelihoods, precluding analytical solutions.
In these cases, we must either: - Use approximations (Laplace, variational inference). - Use MCMC or other sampling methods to draw from \(p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) \propto p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta})\).

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.8 Practical MCMC Fitting Workflow
The following seven-step workflow is the standard approach for fitting regression models via MCMC:

12.8.1 Step 1: Specify the Generative Model and Noise Model
Write down: - The regression function: \(f(x; \boldsymbol{\theta})\). - The noise distribution: e.g., \(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\). - The observation model: \(y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(f(x_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}), \sigma^2)\).


12.8.2 Step 2: Choose Priors for All Parameters
Specify weakly informative priors for each parameter. For regression coefficients, use broad Gaussians (e.g., \(\mathcal{N}(0, 10^2)\)). For scale parameters like \(\sigma\), use a Jeffreys prior \(p(\sigma) \propto 1/\sigma\) or truncated exponentials. To avoid boundary issues, sample on the log-scale: \(\eta = \log \sigma\).

Reparameterization for Bounded Parameters:
Parameters constrained to positive values (like \(\sigma > 0\)) should be sampled in transformed space: - Replace \(\sigma\) with \(\eta = \log \sigma\), so \(\sigma = e^\eta\). - A flat prior on \(\eta\) corresponds to a Jeffreys prior on \(\sigma\). - The Jacobian \(|d\sigma / d\eta| = e^\eta = \sigma\) automatically cancels the \(1/\sigma\) prior, yielding a uniform prior in log-space.
For slope parameters, reparameterize via angle: \(\beta = \tan(\alpha)\), so all slopes are finite and comparable.



12.8.3 Step 3: Write Log-Prior, Log-Likelihood, and Log-Posterior Functions
Define functions that compute: \[\begin{align}
\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &: \text{ sum of log-prior densities for all parameters}, \\
\log p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) &: \text{ log-likelihood, typically } \sum_{i=1}^N \log p(y_i \mid x_i, \boldsymbol{\theta}), \\
\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) &= \log p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) + \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \text{const}.
\end{align}\]
For MCMC, we only need the unnormalized posterior (up to an additive constant).


12.8.4 Step 4: Run MCMC and Tune Proposal Covariance
Initialize parameters at a reasonable starting point (e.g., a classical fit or MAP estimate). Specify a proposal distribution, typically a multivariate Gaussian with tuned covariance. Run MCMC (e.g., random-walk Metropolis-Hastings, or preferably NUTS via Stan) for a burn-in period, then for additional sampling iterations.
Tune the proposal variance to achieve a target acceptance rate (typically 20–30% for random-walk MH, automatic for NUTS).


12.8.5 Step 5: Diagnose and Inspect the Chain

Remark. Key Diagnostics: - Trace plots: Plot \(\theta_j\) versus iteration. Traces should resemble white noise (good mixing), not slow drifts or stuck chains. - Autocorrelation function (ACF): Compute \(\text{Corr}(\theta_j^{(k)}, \theta_j^{(k+\ell)})\) as a function of lag \(\ell\). Rapid decay (small effective sample size if slow decay) indicates good mixing. - Effective sample size (ESS): \(\text{ESS} = N_{\text{samples}} / (1 + 2 \sum_{\ell=1}^\infty \text{Corr}(\ell))\). Rule of thumb: aim for ESS \(> 1000\) per parameter. - \(\hat{R}\) (Gelman-Rubin statistic): Ratio of between-chain to within-chain variance. \(\hat{R} < 1.01\) indicates convergence; \(\hat{R} > 1.05\) indicates poor mixing or insufficient burn-in. - Joint posterior pairs plot: Scatter plot of all pairs of parameters. Reveals correlations and identifies poorly identified parameters.



12.8.6 Step 6: Summarize Parameters
Compute posterior summaries from the converged samples (after thinning to reduce autocorrelation if desired): - Maximum a posteriori (MAP): The sample with highest posterior density. - Posterior mean: Average of all samples. - Posterior covariance: Empirical covariance of samples. - Credible intervals: 2.5th and 97.5th quantiles (or other quantiles) per parameter.
Always transform samples back to the original parameterization before computing summaries.


12.8.7 Step 7: Plot Predictions and Posterior Predictive Checks

Data plot with error bars: Show observed \((x_i, y_i)\) with vertical error bars at \(\pm \sigma_{\text{MAP}}\) or \(\pm\) posterior SD of \(y_i\).
Fitted regression curve: Plot the posterior mean fit \(\hat{f}(x) = \boldsymbol{\phi}(x)^\top \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w} \mid D]\).
Credible band for the mean curve: For a grid of \(x\) values, compute the posterior quantiles of \(f(x; \boldsymbol{\theta})\) by evaluating the function at each posterior sample. This shows uncertainty in the regression function, not including observation noise.
Posterior predictive band: For future observations, include both parameter uncertainty and noise. For each \(x\) on the grid and each posterior sample \(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}\), generate \(y^{(k)} \sim \mathcal{N}(f(x; \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}), \sigma^{(k)2})\). The 2.5th and 97.5th quantiles across samples form the prediction interval.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.9 Example: Linear Regression with MCMC
Consider fitting a straight line \(f(x) = b_0 + b_1 x\) to observed data \(\{(x_i, y_i)\}\), with unknown noise \(\sigma\).
Parameters (transformed): \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (b_0, \alpha, \eta)\) where \(b_1 = \tan(\alpha)\) and \(\eta = \log \sigma\).
Priors: - \(b_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 10^2)\). - \(\alpha \sim \text{Uniform}(-\pi/2, \pi/2)\) (equivalently, \(p(\alpha) \propto 1\)). - \(\eta \sim \text{Uniform}\) (flat prior on \(\log \sigma\), Jeffreys prior on \(\sigma\)).
Log-likelihood: \[\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid b_0, \alpha, \eta) = -N \eta - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{[y_i - (b_0 + \tan(\alpha) x_i)]^2}{e^{2\eta}} + \text{const}.\]
Log-posterior: Sum of log-likelihood and log-prior densities.
Run MCMC (e.g., via random-walk MH or Stan’s NUTS sampler) and diagnose convergence.


Show code
set.seed(42)
N <- 8
x <- seq(0, 7, length.out = N)
sigma_true <- 1.0
y <- 0 + 1 * x + rnorm(N, 0, sigma_true)

plot(x, y, pch = 19, col = "black", xlab = "x", ylab = "y",
     xlim = c(-0.5, 7.5), ylim = c(-2, 9),
     main = "Linear Regression Data")





Linear regression example: 8 data points from y = 0 + x + noise.




Now run the MCMC sampler (pseudocode; in practice, use Stan, JAGS, or a hand-coded sampler):


Show code
# Log-likelihood for y_i ~ N(b0 + b1 * x_i, sigma^2)
log_likelihood <- function(b0, b1, sigma, y, x) {
  mu <- b0 + b1 * x
  sum(dnorm(y, mu, sigma, log = TRUE))
}

# Log-posterior (unnormalized)
log_posterior <- function(theta, data) {
  b0 <- theta[1]
  alpha <- theta[2]
  eta <- theta[3]

  b1 <- tan(alpha)
  sigma <- exp(eta)

  # Priors
  log_prior_b0 <- dnorm(b0, 0, 10, log = TRUE)
  log_prior_alpha <- 0  # Flat in alpha
  log_prior_eta <- 0    # Flat in eta (Jeffreys for sigma)

  # Likelihood
  log_like <- log_likelihood(b0, b1, sigma, data$y, data$x)

  log_like + log_prior_b0 + log_prior_alpha + log_prior_eta
}


After running MCMC for 50,000 iterations (with 10,000 burn-in), extract the posterior samples and compute diagnostics:


Show code
# This is pseudocode; actual implementation depends on your MCMC sampler.
# Assume 'samples' is a matrix with columns: b0, alpha, eta

# Trace plot
par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(3.5, 3.5, 1, 1))
plot(samples[, 1], type = "l", xlab = "iteration", ylab = "b0",
     col = "black", lwd = 0.5)
plot(samples[, 2], type = "l", xlab = "iteration", ylab = "alpha",
     col = "black", lwd = 0.5)
plot(samples[, 3], type = "l", xlab = "iteration", ylab = "log(sigma)",
     col = "black", lwd = 0.5)


After burn-in, summarize the posterior:


Show code
# Transform back to original scale
b0_samp <- samples[, 1]
b1_samp <- tan(samples[, 2])
sigma_samp <- exp(samples[, 3])

# Posterior means and credible intervals
cat("Posterior summaries:\n")
cat("b0:", mean(b0_samp), "± [", quantile(b0_samp, 0.025), ",",
    quantile(b0_samp, 0.975), "]\n")
cat("b1:", mean(b1_samp), "± [", quantile(b1_samp, 0.025), ",",
    quantile(b1_samp, 0.975), "]\n")
cat("sigma:", mean(sigma_samp), "± [", quantile(sigma_samp, 0.025), ",",
    quantile(sigma_samp, 0.975), "]\n")




Show code
# Prediction grid
x_grid <- seq(-0.5, 7.5, length.out = 200)

# Compute posterior mean and predictive curves
f_samples <- matrix(NA, nrow = length(x_grid), ncol = length(b0_samp))
y_pred_samples <- matrix(NA, nrow = length(x_grid), ncol = length(b0_samp))

for (k in seq_along(b0_samp)) {
  f_samples[, k] <- b0_samp[k] + b1_samp[k] * x_grid
  y_pred_samples[, k] <- f_samples[, k] + rnorm(length(x_grid), 0, sigma_samp[k])
}

# Compute quantiles
f_lo <- apply(f_samples, 1, quantile, 0.025)
f_hi <- apply(f_samples, 1, quantile, 0.975)
f_med <- apply(f_samples, 1, median)

y_lo <- apply(y_pred_samples, 1, quantile, 0.025)
y_hi <- apply(y_pred_samples, 1, quantile, 0.975)

# Plot
plot(x, y, pch = 19, col = "black", xlab = "x", ylab = "y",
     xlim = c(-0.5, 7.5), ylim = c(-4, 10),
     main = "Linear Regression Fit with Uncertainty Bands")

# Posterior predictive band (wider, includes noise)
polygon(c(x_grid, rev(x_grid)), c(y_lo, rev(y_hi)),
        col = rgb(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.3), border = NA)

# Credible band for mean (narrower, only parameter uncertainty)
polygon(c(x_grid, rev(x_grid)), c(f_lo, rev(f_hi)),
        col = rgb(0.3, 0.3, 0.3, 0.4), border = NA)

# Posterior mean fit
lines(x_grid, f_med, col = "black", lwd = 2)

# Legend
legend("topleft", c("Data", "Posterior mean", "Credible band", "Predictive band"),
       pch = c(19, NA, NA, NA), lty = c(NA, 1, NA, NA),
       col = c("black", "black", "gray30", "gray50"),
       fill = c(NA, NA, rgb(0.3, 0.3, 0.3, 0.4), rgb(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.3)),
       border = NA)

title: 12  Bayesian regression
section: 12.10 Nonlinear Models: Laplace Approximation and MCMC
text:
12.10 Nonlinear Models: Laplace Approximation and MCMC
When \(f(x; \theta)\) is nonlinear in \(\theta\), the posterior cannot be written in closed form. Two main approaches:
Laplace Approximation: Expand the log-posterior around the MAP estimate \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) as a multivariate Gaussian: \[p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) \approx \mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}, [-\nabla^2 \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) |_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}]^{-1}).\]
This is fast and often accurate for well-behaved posteriors. For multimodal or heavy-tailed posteriors, it can fail.
MCMC: Sample directly from the posterior, handling arbitrary nonlinearity and non-Gaussian shapes. The disadvantage is computational cost; the advantage is generality and accuracy. Modern samplers like NUTS (in Stan) adapt the step size and use Hamiltonian dynamics, making MCMC scalable.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.11 Posterior Predictive Checks
A fundamental tool in Bayesian regression is posterior predictive checking: generate new data under the posterior and compare to observed data.

For a regression model, the posterior predictive distribution is \[p(y_{\text{new}} \mid x_{\text{new}}, D) = \int p(y_{\text{new}} \mid x_{\text{new}}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta}.\]
The marginal predictive distribution averages over parameter uncertainty, automatically accounting for both the fitted model and the noise in the data.
Posterior predictive checks: 1. For each posterior sample \(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}\), generate \(y_{\text{rep}}^{(k)}(x_i) \sim p(y \mid x_i, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)})\) at each observed \(x_i\). 2. Compare the distribution of simulated \(y_{\text{rep}}\) to observed \(y\) using summary statistics (e.g., mean, variance, quantiles, or max absolute residual). 3. If the posterior predictive distribution is consistent with the observed data, the model is plausible.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.12 Robustness and Extensions

12.12.1 Student-\(t\) Noise for Robustness
Gaussian noise is sensitive to outliers. A robust alternative is Student-\(t\) noise: \[y_i \sim t_\nu(f(x_i; \theta), \sigma^2),\] where \(\nu\) controls tail heaviness (small \(\nu\) allows larger outliers). As \(\nu \to \infty\), recovers Gaussian. Often set \(\nu = 3\) or \(\nu = 4\) for robustness.
The likelihood becomes \[p(y_i \mid \ldots) \propto \left[ 1 + \frac{[y_i - f(x_i; \theta)]^2}{\nu \sigma^2} \right]^{-(\nu+1)/2},\] which is heavier-tailed than Gaussian. MCMC is needed to fit such models.


12.12.2 Errors in All Variables
If both \(x_i\) and \(y_i\) are measured with error, the problem becomes significantly harder: \[x_i = x_i^{\text{true}} + \delta_{x,i}, \quad y_i = f(x_i^{\text{true}}; \theta) + \delta_{y,i}.\]
The true \(x_i^{\text{true}}\) must be treated as latent variables, increasing dimensionality. Bayesian approaches handle this naturally via hierarchical modeling, but require additional priors and tuning.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.13 Summary
Bayesian regression provides a principled framework for learning functional relationships:

Linear models (parameters enter linearly) admit conjugate closed-form posteriors via Gaussian theory, enabling exact inference and efficient prediction.
Posterior prediction integrates over parameter uncertainty, yielding predictive distributions that account for both fitted function and observation noise.
Nonlinear and generalized models (nonlinear parameters, non-Gaussian likelihoods) require MCMC sampling or approximations.
Practical MCMC workflow (specify model, choose priors, write log-posterior, run sampler, diagnose, summarize, plot) is systematic and reproducible.
Posterior predictive checks verify model adequacy and reveal misfit.
Robustness (heavy-tailed noise) and latent variable models (measurement error) extend the framework to real data.

The same logic applies across simple linear regression, polynomial fits, nonlinear curves, and generalized linear models: only the parameterization and likelihood change; the Bayesian template—prior \(\times\) likelihood $/ $ evidence—remains constant.

text:
12.14 Sources

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.1 Introduction: from hand-coded to declarative models
In preceding chapters, we implemented Markov chain Monte Carlo by hand: we wrote log-likelihood and log-prior functions, tuned proposal covariance matrices, diagnosed convergence, and iterated. This approach is powerful pedagogically—it reveals exactly what is being sampled and how—but it is labour-intensive and error-prone for realistic models.
Real-world inference often requires many hyperparameters, hierarchical structure, and thousands of latent variables. Writing the full log-posterior by hand for such models is impractical. We need a language in which to declare the model in terms of its statistical structure, and delegate the numerical sampling to software.
Probabilistic programming languages (PPLs) solve this. They allow you to write: \[\text{model + data} \to p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y}).\]
Two mainstream PPLs in the applied Bayesian community are JAGS and Stan. We also cover variational inference, an alternative to MCMC that solves the posterior by optimisation, and its automation in ADVI (automatic differentiation variational inference).
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.2 The classical limitation: conjugacy
In the first chapters of this course, we exploited conjugate priors: when the prior and likelihood share a specific functional form, the posterior is tractable analytically. For example, a Beta prior on a Bernoulli probability yields a Beta posterior; a Normal prior on a Gaussian mean (with known variance) yields a Normal posterior.
Conjugacy is rare in practice. Most real models are non-conjugate: the posterior is not available in closed form. In such cases we have two paths:

MCMC: Sample from the posterior.
Variational inference: Approximate the posterior with a tractable family.

Both JAGS and Stan use MCMC. JAGS uses Gibbs sampling or Metropolis-within-Gibbs (no gradient information). Stan uses Hamiltonian Monte Carlo with automatic differentiation (gradient-based), which mixes much faster.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
section: 13.3 JAGS: Gibbs sampling with a model syntax
text:
13.3 JAGS: Gibbs sampling with a model syntax

13.3.1 Overview
JAGS stands for Just Another Gibbs Sampler. It is a language and engine for specifying Bayesian hierarchical models and sampling from them using Gibbs updates or Metropolis-Hastings steps.
The key idea: you write the model in a declarative syntax that lists the conditional distribution of each variable given its parents. JAGS parses this, determines which variables can be sampled by Gibbs and which need Metropolis-Hastings, and executes the sampler automatically.


13.3.2 Model syntax
A JAGS model comprises three optional blocks: data, model, and inits.
The data block declares observed quantities (fixed).
The model block specifies the generative structure: for each stochastic node (parameter, latent variable, observation), you write its conditional distribution.
The inits block (usually separate in R) provides starting values.
Example: centered quadratic regression.
model {
  # Likelihood
  for (i in 1:N) {
    y[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], tau)
    mu[i] <- a0 + a1 * z[i] + a2 * pow(z[i], 2)
  }

  # Priors
  a0 ~ dnorm(0, 1/100)
  a1 ~ dnorm(0, 1/100)
  a2 ~ dnorm(0, 1/100)

  # Precision parameterization
  tau <- 1 / pow(sigma, 2)
  sigma ~ dunif(0, 20)
}
Key syntax notes:

JAGS uses precision \(\tau = 1/\sigma^2\), not standard deviation.
Deterministic transformations use <-; stochastic nodes use ~.
Distributions are prefixed d: dnorm, dunif, dbinom, etc.
The prior on \(\sigma\) must be proper.



13.3.3 Workflow in R with rjags
library(rjags)
library(coda)

# Prepare data
jags_data <- list(
  N = nrow(obsdata),
  z = obsdata$z,
  y = obsdata$y
)

# Initialize
inits <- function() list(a0=0, a1=0, a2=0, sigma=1)

# Compile
jm <- jags.model(textConnection(model_string),
                 data=jags_data,
                 inits=inits,
                 n.chains=4)

# Adapt (burn-in)
update(jm, 2000)

# Sample
samp <- coda.samples(jm,
                     variable.names=c("a0","a1","a2","sigma"),
                     n.iter=20000)

# Summarize
summary(samp)
effectiveSize(samp)
Advantages of JAGS:

Simple declarative syntax.
Automatic Gibbs / MH switching.
Handles discrete and continuous parameters.

Disadvantages:

Gibbs sampling mixes slowly for correlated posteriors.
No gradient information; cannot exploit manifold geometry.
Precision parameterization less intuitive than standard deviation.

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
section: 13.6 Variational inference: approximate posterior via optimisation
text:
13.6 Variational inference: approximate posterior via optimisation

13.6.1 The KL divergence decomposition
MCMC samples exactly (asymptotically) from the posterior \(p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y})\). Variational inference instead optimises over a family of tractable distributions \(\mathcal{Q}\) to find the member \(q^*(\theta)\) that best approximates the posterior.
Define the Kullback–Leibler divergence: \[\mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p) = \mathbb{E}_q \left[ \log \frac{q(\theta)}{p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y})} \right].\]
By the law of total probability, \[\log p(\mathbf{y}) = \mathbb{E}_q [ \log p(\mathbf{y}, \theta) - \log q(\theta) ] + \mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p),\]
which rearranges to
\[\boxed{\log p(\mathbf{y}) = \mathcal{L}(q) + \mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p)}.\]
Here \(\mathcal{L}(q)\) is the evidence lower bound (ELBO): \[\mathcal{L}(q) = \mathbb{E}_q [ \log p(\mathbf{y}, \theta) - \log q(\theta) ].\]
Since \(\log p(\mathbf{y})\) is fixed and \(\mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p) \geq 0\) always, maximising the ELBO minimises the KL divergence. Thus the optimal approximation is
\[q^*(\theta) = \arg\max_{q \in \mathcal{Q}} \mathcal{L}(q).\]


13.6.2 Mean-field approximation
To make the optimisation tractable, we restrict \(\mathcal{Q}\) to a mean-field family: \[q(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{d} q_i(\theta_i),\]
i.e., a product of independent marginal distributions (one per parameter). This destroys posterior correlations but is far easier to optimise than a full covariance Gaussian.
For each parameter, \(q_i\) is typically a standard distribution (Gaussian, Beta, Gamma, etc.).


13.6.3 The reparameterisation trick
To optimise the ELBO by stochastic gradient descent, we need to differentiate through expectations. The reparameterisation trick rewrites an expectation: \[\mathbb{E}_{q(\theta)} [f(\theta)] = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon \sim N(0, I)} [f(\mu + \sigma \epsilon)],\]
where \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) are the (optimisable) parameters of \(q\), and \(\epsilon\) is auxiliary randomness. Now we can differentiate w.r.t. \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) using the chain rule.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.7 ADVI: Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference

13.7.1 Automatic transformation to unconstrained space
Many parameters have constraints: \(\sigma > 0\), \(p \in [0, 1]\), etc. ADVI automatically transforms such parameters to an unconstrained space, optimises a Gaussian approximation there, and back-transforms samples.
For example, \(\sigma > 0\) is mapped to \(\eta = \log \sigma \in \mathbb{R}\). The variational family becomes \(q(\eta)\) (a univariate Gaussian in \(\eta\)), and we sample \(\eta \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\), then set \(\sigma = \exp(\eta)\).


13.7.2 Gaussian variational family
ADVI uses a multivariate Gaussian approximation: \[q(\theta) = N(\theta \mid \mu, \Sigma),\]
where \(\Sigma\) is either diagonal (mean-field) or full-rank. The parameters \((\mu, \Sigma)\) are optimised by stochastic gradient ascent on the ELBO.


13.7.3 Stan’s vb() interface
# Variational inference in Stan (instead of MCMC)
fit_vb <- mod$variational(
  data = stan_data,
  algorithm = "meanfield",  # or "fullrank"
  iter = 10000,
  seed = 123
)

# Approximately posterior samples
draws <- fit_vb$draws()
Advantages of ADVI:

Much faster than MCMC for high-dimensional models.
Deterministic; no convergence diagnostics needed.
Useful for exploratory analysis.

Disadvantages:

Mean-field assumption often too restrictive; underestimates posterior variance.
No automatic diagnostic (unlike MCMC diagnostics like \(\hat{R}\)).
For posterior inference, MCMC is often preferable.

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
section: 13.8 Practical workflow: from hand-rolled MCMC to PPLs
text:
13.8 Practical workflow: from hand-rolled MCMC to PPLs
The progression we have traced reflects a real scientific workflow:

Prototype with hand-coded MCMC (Chapter 11 onwards): Learn the model, understand the geometry of the posterior, diagnose what works and what doesn’t.
Apply parametrisation tricks: Center and scale predictors, use whitening transforms, exploit posterior covariance structure.
Upgrade to a gradient-based sampler: Use HMC or NUTS to exploit smoothness of the log-posterior.
Automate with Stan or JAGS: Declare the model once, let the software handle sampling, diagnostics, and adaptation.
Consider variational inference if:

You need very fast approximate inference for exploration.
Your model is too large for MCMC.
You are happy accepting mean-field correlations.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.9 Failure modes and remedies

13.9.1 Multimodal posteriors
If the target distribution has multiple distinct modes, MCMC may get stuck in one mode and miss others. JAGS and Stan both struggle with multimodality.
Remedies:

Use parallel tempering (Chapter 10).
Run many short chains from dispersed starting values.
Reparametrise to reduce multimodality (e.g., via identifiability constraints).



13.9.2 Discrete parameters
Stan requires all parameters to be continuous. If your model includes discrete latent variables (e.g., mixture component labels), you must marginalise them out analytically (integrate over all possible values) before passing the model to Stan.
JAGS handles discrete nodes naturally.


13.9.3 Heavy-tailed posteriors
If the posterior has heavy tails (e.g., a mixture of normals with small weights), the leapfrog integrator in HMC can take very long steps and diverge. Divergent transitions are a warning.
Remedies:

Increase adapt_delta (0.8 → 0.95 or higher).
Increase max_treedepth.
Reparametrise to reduce tail heaviness (e.g., use a Student-\(t\) prior to allow more dispersed posteriors).



13.9.4 Weak identifiability
If two parameters affect the likelihood in nearly the same way, the posterior is highly correlated or even degenerate. MCMC mixes slowly; variational inference underestimates uncertainty.
Remedy:

Include informative priors that break the symmetry.
Reparametrise (e.g., use a sum-of-effects rather than separate effects).
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.10 What to report
For any Bayesian analysis using JAGS or Stan, report:

Model specification: Likelihood, priors, parametrisation.
Sampling method: Algorithm, number of chains, iterations, warmup.
Convergence diagnostics: Trace plots, \(\hat{R}\), ESS, acceptance rate, divergent transitions (Stan only).
Posterior summaries: Mean or median, 95% credible intervals.
Posterior predictions: Regression curves with credible intervals; posterior predictive intervals if relevant.

Do not report precise posterior point estimates from a badly mixing chain—they are numerical artifacts, not inference.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.1 Introduction: When linear regression fails
Classical Gaussian linear regression assumes that the response variable \(y_i\) is continuous and approximately normally distributed with mean \(\mu_i = f(x_i; \beta)\) and constant variance \(\sigma^2\). This assumption works well for many physical measurements with small, additive noise.
However, in many experiments we encounter response variables that violate this assumption fundamentally:
Fitting a standard linear model \(y_i \sim N(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i, \sigma^2)\) to such data produces:
Generalized linear models extend regression to non-Gaussian responses by replacing the normality assumption with a member of the exponential family and introducing a link function that maps the linear predictor onto the valid parameter space.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
Count data: number of detected photons in a time window, decay events in a bin, or success counts in repeated trials. These are non-negative integers with variance proportional to the mean, not constant.
Binary outcomes: detection (yes/no), selection success (pass/fail), or signal presence (present/absent). The mean must lie in \((0,1)\) to represent a probability.
Positive-only data: time-to-event, energy, or rate parameters. The mean must be strictly positive.



Invalid predictions: fitted means may fall outside the natural parameter space (e.g., negative counts or probabilities outside \([0,1]\)).
Incorrect uncertainty quantification: constant-variance assumptions badly misrepresent the variability structure of heteroscedastic responses.
Biased inference: parameter estimates become inefficient and confidence intervals lose their intended coverage.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.2 The three ingredients of a GLM
text:
14.2 The three ingredients of a GLM
A generalized linear model comprises three interconnected components:

Definition (GLM structure): A generalized linear model for observations \(y_1, \ldots, y_N\) is specified by:

Random component: \(y_i \mid \theta_i \sim p(y_i; \theta_i)\), where \(p\) belongs to an exponential-family distribution parameterized by a natural parameter \(\theta_i\).
Systematic component: a linear predictor \[\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots + \beta_p x_{i,p} = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta},\] where \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is a vector of regression coefficients and \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the design vector for observation \(i\).
Link function: a smooth, invertible function \(g(\cdot)\) that relates the mean \(\mu_i = \mathbb{E}[y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i]\) to the linear predictor: \[g(\mu_i) = \eta_i, \quad \text{equivalently} \quad \mu_i = g^{-1}(\eta_i).\] The inverse link, \(g^{-1}\), is called the mean function.


The link function ensures that even though \(\eta_i\) can take any real value, the predicted mean \(\mu_i = g^{-1}(\eta_i)\) respects the constraints of the response distribution.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.3 The exponential family and canonical form
text:
14.3 The exponential family and canonical form
The exponential family is a large class of distributions sharing a common parameterization that unifies likelihood inference for GLMs.

Definition (Exponential family canonical form): A probability distribution for \(y\) belongs to the exponential family if it can be written as \[p(y; \theta, \phi) = h(y, \phi) \exp\left(\frac{\theta y - A(\theta)}{\phi}\right),\] where: - \(\theta\) is the natural parameter (or canonical parameter). - \(\phi > 0\) is the dispersion parameter, often related to variance. - \(A(\theta)\) is the cumulant function or log-partition function. - \(h(y, \phi)\) is the base measure (carries \(y\)-dependent normalization and \(\phi\) terms not involving \(\theta\)).
The term \(A(\theta)\) ensures the distribution integrates or sums to one.


Theorem (Moment relations): For a distribution in exponential-family form, \[\mathbb{E}[y] = A'(\theta), \quad \mathrm{Var}(y) = \phi \cdot A''(\theta).\]
Proof sketch: The log-likelihood is \(\ell(\theta; y) = \theta y - A(\theta) + \text{const}\). The score is \(\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = y - A'(\theta)\). Setting \(\mathbb{E}[\text{score}] = 0\) gives \(\mathbb{E}[y] = A'(\theta)\). The variance follows from the Hessian of \(\ell\).

These moment relations are powerful: they immediately connect the natural parameter \(\theta\) to the mean \(\mu\) and relate the mean to the variance through \(A''(\theta)\).

title: 14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.4 Canonical links and the link-function table
text:
14.4 Canonical links and the link-function table
A canonical link is the link function such that \(g(\mu) = \theta\). For canonical links, the natural parameter equals the linear predictor directly, which simplifies both interpretation and computation (the sufficient statistics for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) are linear in the data).
The following table summarizes the most common GLM families used in practice:

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.5 Logistic regression: Bernoulli response

14.5.1 Model and likelihood
Logistic regression models a binary response \(y_i \in \{0, 1\}\) as a Bernoulli trial: \[y_i \mid \pi_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\pi_i),\] where \(\pi_i = \Pr(y_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_i)\) is the success probability.
The Bernoulli distribution has mean \(\mu_i = \pi_i\) and variance \(\pi_i(1 - \pi_i)\). The cumulant function is \(A(\theta) = \log(1 + e^\theta)\), so the canonical link is the logit: \[g(\pi_i) = \log\frac{\pi_i}{1 - \pi_i} = \eta_i = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}.\]
The inverse logit or logistic function recovers the probability: \[\pi_i = \sigma(\eta_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\eta_i}}.\]
The likelihood for \(N\) independent observations is \[p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i^{y_i} (1 - \pi_i)^{1 - y_i},\] with log-likelihood \[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}; D) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \eta_i - \log(1 + e^{\eta_i}) \right].\]


14.5.2 Interpretation: log-odds and odds ratios
The logit transforms the probability \(\pi\) into the log-odds. The odds are \(\frac{\pi}{1-\pi}\), the ratio of success to failure probability. Logistic regression is linear on the log-odds scale: \[\log\frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots.\]
A one-unit increase in \(x_j\) changes the log-odds by \(\beta_j\), or equivalently, multiplies the odds by \(e^{\beta_j}\). If \(\beta_1 = 0.5\), a one-unit increase in \(x_1\) multiplies the odds of success by \(e^{0.5} \approx 1.65\).
The change in probability is nonlinear: the same increase in \(x\) produces a larger change in \(\pi\) near \(\pi = 0.5\) and smaller changes near 0 or 1.


14.5.3 Logistic function visualization


Show code
eta <- seq(-6, 6, length.out = 200)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-eta))

plot(eta, pi, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "black",
     xlab = expression(eta), ylab = expression(pi),
     ylim = c(0, 1), las = 1)
grid(col = "gray80")
abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "gray40", lwd = 1.5)
abline(v = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray40", lwd = 1.5)





The logistic function \(\sigma(\eta) = 1/(1+\exp(-\eta))\).

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.6 Poisson regression: Count response

14.6.1 Model and likelihood
Poisson regression models count data \(y_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}\) as a Poisson distribution: \[y_i \mid \lambda_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i),\] where \(\lambda_i > 0\) is the expected count or rate. Both mean and variance equal \(\lambda_i\): \(\mathbb{E}[y_i] = \mathrm{Var}(y_i) = \lambda_i\).
The cumulant function is \(A(\theta) = e^\theta\), and the canonical link is the log link: \[g(\lambda_i) = \log \lambda_i = \eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots,\] so \(\lambda_i = e^{\eta_i}\).
The Poisson likelihood is \[p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\lambda_i^{y_i} e^{-\lambda_i}}{y_i!},\] with log-likelihood \[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}; D) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \lambda_i - \lambda_i - \log(y_i!) \right].\]


14.6.2 Interpretation: exponential effects
The log link means that coefficients have exponential interpretation. A one-unit increase in \(x_j\) multiplies the expected count by \(e^{\beta_j}\): \[\frac{\lambda(x_j + 1)}{\lambda(x_j)} = e^{\beta_j}.\]
If \(\beta_1 = 0.3\), then increasing \(x_1\) by one unit multiplies the expected count by \(e^{0.3} \approx 1.35\), a 35% increase.


14.6.3 Offsets and exposure
In many applications, observations have different exposure times or bin widths. For example, detector data collected over different durations \(E_i\). The expected count scales with exposure: \(\lambda_i = E_i r_i\), where \(r_i\) is the event rate.
Using the log link, \[\log \lambda_i = \log(E_i r_i) = \log E_i + \log r_i = \log E_i + \eta_i,\] where \(\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots\). The term \(\log E_i\) is a known offset, a coefficient fixed at 1.
In Stan or R, specify log(exposure) + w0 + w1*x in the linear predictor; the software interprets the known contribution separately.


14.6.4 Simulated Poisson regression example


Show code
set.seed(101)
N <- 30
x <- seq(0, 5, length.out = N)
w0_true <- 0.5
w1_true <- 0.35
eta <- w0_true + w1_true * x
lambda <- exp(eta)
y <- rpois(N, lambda = lambda)

plot(x, y, pch = 19, col = "black", xlab = "x", ylab = "count",
     ylim = c(0, max(y) * 1.1), cex = 0.9, las = 1)
lines(x, lambda, lwd = 2.5, col = "#b8123e")
grid(col = "gray80")
legend("topleft", legend = c("observed", "true rate"),
       pch = c(19, NA), lty = c(NA, 1), lwd = c(NA, 2.5),
       col = c("black", "#b8123e"), bty = "n")





Simulated Poisson regression data and fitted rate curve.






14.6.5 Overdispersion and negative binomial extension
A fundamental property of the Poisson is that variance equals the mean. In real count data, the variance often exceeds the mean—a phenomenon called overdispersion. Causes include:

Unobserved heterogeneity: different subgroups have different rates.
Clustering: observations within groups are not independent.
Extra-Poisson variability: a genuine excess beyond Poisson noise.


Overdispersion test: Compute \(\hat{\lambda}\) from fitted Poisson regression. If the sample variance of residuals \(\sum_i (y_i - \hat{\lambda}_i)^2 / (N - p)\) is much larger than the average fitted rate, overdispersion is present.

The negative binomial distribution extends Poisson regression by adding a shape parameter \(\phi\) that allows variance to exceed the mean: \[\mathrm{Var}(y_i) = \lambda_i + \frac{\lambda_i^2}{\phi}.\] As \(\phi \to \infty\), the negative binomial approaches Poisson. A negative-binomial GLM is fitted similarly to Poisson but with an additional dispersion parameter to estimate.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.7 Illustration: link functions
The choice of link function transforms the linear predictor onto the appropriate scale. Here we compare logit (for Bernoulli) and log (for Poisson) side by side:


Show code
eta_seq <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 200)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Logit (inverse)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-eta_seq))
plot(eta_seq, pi, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#b8123e",
     xlab = expression(eta), ylab = expression(mu),
     main = "Logit inverse link", las = 1)
grid(col = "gray80")

# Log (inverse)
lambda <- exp(eta_seq)
plot(eta_seq, lambda, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#b8123e",
     xlab = expression(eta), ylab = expression(mu),
     main = "Log inverse link", ylim = c(0, 55), las = 1)
grid(col = "gray80")





Canonical link functions: logit maps \(\eta\) to \((0,1)\) for probabilities; log maps \(\eta\) to \((0, \infty)\) for rates.




Show code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: 14  Generalized linear models
14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.9 Residual diagnostics and posterior predictive checks
text:
14.9 Residual diagnostics and posterior predictive checks

14.9.1 Deviance and residuals
In a fitted GLM, the deviance is twice the log-likelihood difference between the saturated model (one parameter per observation) and the fitted model: \[D = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \ell_{\text{sat}}(y_i) - \ell_{\text{fitted}}(\hat{\mu}_i, y_i) \right].\]
For logistic regression, this simplifies to a sum of log-likelihood comparisons; for Poisson, it involves Kullback–Leibler divergence between observed and fitted counts.
Pearson residuals scale differences by the estimated standard deviation: \[r_i^{\text{Pearson}} = \frac{y_i - \hat{\mu}_i}{\sqrt{\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(y_i)}}.\]
In well-fitting models, Pearson residuals should be approximately standard normal. Large residuals flag individual observations that the model does not explain well.


14.9.2 Posterior predictive checks
In Bayesian inference, we can generate replicated data \(y_{\text{rep}}^{(s)}\) from the posterior predictive distribution for each MCMC draw \((s)\). By comparing summaries of observed data \(y\) to summaries of replicated data, we can assess whether the model is plausible:

Poisson: Check the proportion of zeros, the maximum count, the ratio of variance to mean.
Bernoulli: Check the total number of successes, the success fraction in subgroups, the patterns of runs.

If the observed summary is consistently in the tail of the replicated-data distribution, the model misses important structure.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.10 Coefficient interpretation and prediction

14.10.1 Logistic regression coefficients
In logistic regression with predictor \(x_j\), a one-unit increase in \(x_j\) (holding others fixed) changes the odds by a factor of \(e^{\beta_j}\): - If \(\beta_j = 0.5\), the odds increase by a factor of \(e^{0.5} \approx 1.65\) (65% increase). - If \(\beta_j = -0.3\), the odds decrease by a factor of \(e^{-0.3} \approx 0.74\) (26% decrease).
The corresponding probability change depends on the current probability level. Near \(\pi = 0.5\) (maximum sensitivity), a one-unit change in \(x_j\) shifts \(\pi\) by approximately \(\beta_j / 4\). Near extreme probabilities, the shift is much smaller.
Always report uncertainty: credible intervals for \(\beta_j\) quantify the precision of the estimate. Transform endpoints through \(e^{\beta_j}\) to report odds ratios and their intervals.


14.10.2 Poisson regression coefficients
In Poisson regression, \(\beta_j\) is the change in log rate per unit increase in \(x_j\). The expected count is multiplied by \(e^{\beta_j}\) per unit increase: - If \(\beta_1 = 0.3\), a one-unit increase in \(x_1\) multiplies the expected count by \(e^{0.3} \approx 1.35\) (35% increase). - If \(\beta_2 = -0.2\), a one-unit increase in \(x_2\) multiplies the expected count by \(e^{-0.2} \approx 0.82\) (18% decrease).
These are relative effects: they describe multiplicative changes in the rate, not absolute changes. In a detector experiment, a 35% increase in count rate might be substantial or negligible depending on the baseline and the experimental goal.


14.10.3 Posterior predictive intervals
After fitting, we typically want to predict for new observations. For logistic regression, posterior draws of \((w_0, w_1)\) give posterior samples of \(\pi_* = \sigma(w_0 + w_1 x_*)\) at a new location \(x_*\). The credible interval for \(\pi_*\) quantifies uncertainty in the probability; a separate predictive interval addresses the binary outcome itself (\(y_* \in \{0, 1\}\) with some probability).
For Poisson regression, the posterior predictive interval for a new count \(y_*\) at \(x_*\) involves: 1. Drawing \((w_0^{(s)}, w_1^{(s)})\) from the posterior. 2. Computing \(\lambda_*^{(s)} = \exp(w_0^{(s)} + w_1^{(s)} x_*)\). 3. Drawing \(y_*^{(s)} \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_*^{(s)})\). 4. Summarizing the empirical distribution of \(y_*^{(s)}\) by quantiles (e.g., 2.5%, 50%, 97.5%).
The predictive interval is discrete and generally asymmetric, reflecting the discrete and skewed nature of Poisson noise.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.11 Overdispersion illustration: simulated data with variance inflation
text:
14.11 Overdispersion illustration: simulated data with variance inflation


Show code
set.seed(303)
N <- 100
x <- seq(0, 4, length.out = N)
lambda <- 2 + 3 * x

# Poisson: variance = mean
y_poisson <- rpois(N, lambda = lambda)

# Overdispersed: inflate variance by factor of 2
y_overdispersed <- sapply(lambda, function(mu) {
  nb_size <- mu / 1  # size parameter for negative binomial
  rnbinom(1, size = nb_size, mu = mu)
})

plot(x, y_poisson, pch = 19, col = "gray40", cex = 0.8,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "count", las = 1, ylim = c(0, 30))
points(x, y_overdispersed, pch = 1, col = "#b8123e", cex = 0.8)
lines(x, lambda, lwd = 2, col = "black")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Poisson (Var=mean)", "Overdispersed (Var>mean)", "true mean"),
       pch = c(19, 1, NA), lty = c(NA, NA, 1), col = c("gray40", "#b8123e", "black"),
       bty = "n")





Poisson data (dashed line, Var = mean) vs. overdispersed count data (solid line, Var >> mean).

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.12 Common pitfalls and best practices

Common mistakes:

Ignoring distributional mismatch: Fitting Gaussian regression to counts or binary data leads to invalid predictions and misaligned uncertainty.
Forgetting the offset: In exposure-weighted Poisson models, omitting the offset biases rate estimates and inflates uncertainty.
Misinterpreting coefficients: Logistic coefficients are log-odds, not probability changes. Poisson coefficients are log-rates, not expected count changes.
Reporting point estimates without credible intervals: GLM coefficients are estimated quantities; always include uncertainty via posterior quantiles.
Confusing credible and predictive intervals: The credible interval for \(\mu\) (or \(\pi\), \(\lambda\)) reflects estimation uncertainty. The predictive interval for \(y\) also includes observation noise and is wider.
Ignoring posterior predictive checks: A high likelihood value does not guarantee model adequacy. Generate replicated data and compare to observed summaries.
Trusting diagnostics without thought: High \(\hat{R}\) and large ESS are necessary but not sufficient. Check trace plots, residual patterns, and posterior predictive checks for deeper assessment.



Remark. Best practices:

Center or standardize predictors before fitting to reduce collinearity and improve MCMC mixing. A unit increase in a centered \(x\) is meaningful and comparable across covariates.
Visualize the fitted curve with uncertainty bands overlaid on the data. This reveals whether the model captures the dominant patterns.
Use weakly informative priors that regularize without imposing strong prior beliefs. For coefficients on a standardized scale, \(\text{Normal}(0, 2)\) or \(\text{Normal}(0, 3)\) is reasonable.
Check trace plots for all parameters after sampling. Chains should mix well (no trending, not stuck, high autocorrelation).
Report both point and interval estimates and be explicit about the scale (log-odds, log-rate, probability, count).
Document the model specification (link function, prior, offset, if any) to enable reproducibility and interpretation.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.13 Conclusion
Generalized linear models extend Bayesian regression to count, binary, and other non-Gaussian responses by:

Specifying a response distribution from the exponential family (Poisson, Bernoulli, etc.).
Connecting the mean to a linear predictor via a link function that respects parameter constraints.
Inferring the linear coefficients while propagating uncertainty through the posterior.

The three ingredients—random component, linear predictor, and link function—unify a diverse set of regression tasks under one framework. Logistic regression models probabilities; Poisson regression models rates and counts; gamma and inverse-Gaussian regressions model positive continuous data. MCMC and probabilistic programming allow flexible, fully Bayesian inference, with posterior checks guiding model refinement.
